subject: Ensuring safe, welcoming and inclusive campuses
body:
Important message about expected behaviours at UNSW
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Ensuring safe, welcoming and inclusive campuses
Friday 4 October 2024
Dear Matthew
We continue to acknowledge the immense impact that the devastating loss of life and displacement of people in the Middle East is having on many within our UNSW community.
We are deeply concerned by the rise in disrespectful and at times vilifying public discourse surrounding international conflicts. We would like to reiterate that such behaviour has no place at UNSW and remind every person in our community to behave in a respectful, considerate manner towards their peers and colleagues at this sensitive time, and always.
Ensuring our campuses are safe, welcoming and inclusive environments remains our highest priority. It is every person’s responsibility to make UNSW a place where every student, member of staff and visitor feels and is safe and valued.
We ask that everyone in our community take time to revisit our
Code of Conduct and Values
and the standards of behaviour expected at UNSW. Breaches of the Code will be managed by the University in accordance with relevant policies and procedures, which may include disciplinary action. If we believe breaches of the law have occurred, we will refer the matter to the appropriate external agencies.
We are currently investigating complaints arising from a student meeting on our Kensington campus on 25 September. This was not a UNSW or Arc@UNSW arranged event. The event included behaviour that did not meet our expectations for ensuring an inclusive, safe, and respectful UNSW for everyone. Persons who are found to have engaged in this behaviour will be dealt with in accordance with the
UNSW Complaints Management and Investigations Policy and Procedure.
Academic freedom and freedom of speech are cherished at our university, and UNSW protects freedom of speech wherever people express their views. It is crucial, however, that these freedoms are expressed in compliance with the law. We acknowledge that this has been a very difficult time for many people. With local and global tensions heightened, we ask our community to show respect and care for each other.
Support available to UNSW students
We understand this is a very distressing time for many in our community. Please remember that UNSW has a range of support services available for students, including the
Student Support Team
.
If your ability to study has been impacted in some way by these events, please apply for
Special Consideration
as soon as possible.
If you feel unsafe on campus or you have seen or experienced actions that may make you feel unsafe report via the
SafeZone app
or by calling [PHONE]. You can also SpeakUp and make a complaint (anonymous or otherwise) using our
secure complaints portal
.
Below are some additional mental health support resources available to you.
We will continue to take all reasonable steps to ensure that the culture at UNSW is one of respect, and where academic freedom and freedom of speech are valued alongside the diversity of backgrounds and experiences that are an invaluable feature of our community.
Best regards
Professor Sarah Maddison
Deputy Vice-Chancellor, Education and Student Experience
Mental Health Support Services
If you’re not feeling like your usual self, if you’re feeling stressed, nervous, anxious, or depressed and would like to speak to someone about your mental health, support is available 24/7. Here are your options...
To speak to someone after hours or on the weekend:
Direct counsellor support is available after hours via text
[PHONE]
. For non-urgent 24/7 mental health support, call
[PHONE]
to speak with someone.
For peer-to-peer support:
You can chat to someone online through the
TalkCampus App
.
To speak to someone outside UNSW 24/7:
Lifeline:
13 11 14
Lifeline Live Chat
Beyond Blue:
1300 22 4636
If you or someone you are with is in immediate danger, please call 000, or go to your nearest emergency department immediately.
To make an appointment:
Complete the confidential UNSW
Mental Health Connect
form (the form is available 9.00am-4.30pm, Monday to Friday).
Once you have completed the form, a Mental Health Connect Clinician will contact you to discuss your needs further and suggest options for supports. Recommendations are made based on your individual circumstances and may include internal and/or external ref [ORDER_ID].

subject: Amazon's upcoming competition for students
body:
# **Introducing the Amazon Trusted AI Challenge**
#### **University students will compete for cash prizes in a competition to securely advance LLMs that code.**
Amazon is announcing the Amazon Trusted AI Challenge, a global university competition to drive secure innovation in generative AI technology. This year’s challenge focuses on [responsible AI](
[URL:amazon.science]
"
[URL:amazon.science]
") and
specifically on large language model ([LLM](
[URL:amazon.science]
"
[URL:amazon.science]
"))
coding security.
“We are focusing on advancing the capabilities of coding LLMs, exploring new techniques to automatically identify possible vulnerabilities and effectively secure these models,” said Rohit Prasad, senior vice president and head
scientist, Amazon AGI. “The goal of the Amazon Trusted AI Challenge is to see how students' innovations can help forge a future where generative AI is consistently developed in a way that maintains trust, while highlighting effective methods for safeguarding
LLMs against misuse to enhance their security.”
University students will compete in a tournament-style challenge as either model developer teams or red teams to enhance the AI user experience, prevent misuse, and enable users to build more secure code. Model developer teams
will build security features into code-generating models, while red teams will develop automated techniques to test these models. Each round will allow teams to refine their models and techniques based on multi-turn interactions, identifying strengths and
weaknesses.
Amazon will select up to 10 teams for the competition starting November 2024, which will run through the academic year. Each of the 10 selected teams will receive $250,000 in sponsorship along with monthly AWS credits, and winning
teams have a chance to win an additional $700,000 in cash prizes.
**Advancements and opportunities in AI-assisted software development**
The Amazon Trusted AI Challenge aims to enhance the safety, reliability, and trustworthiness of LLMs powering AI-assisted software development tools. With the rise of generative AI coding assistants, these technologies demonstrate
unprecedented innovative capabilities and offer exciting opportunities to ensure responsible and reliable use. This challenge looks to inspire developers, scientists and researchers to create solutions that enhance AI-assisted coding tools' ability to protect
users and systems.
**Tournament structure**
Through four tournaments and a live finals event, red teams will test model developer teams' AI models to uncover vulnerabilities and improve their security. Red teams will be ranked on their success in forcing models to breach
their policies through automated conversational red-teaming. Model developer teams will create code-generating models to enhance security, identify threats, and prevent unintended behavior. They will be ranked on their ability to build and reinforce successful
defenses through techniques such as fine-tuning and alignment. The goal is to discover innovative ways for LLM creators to mitigate risks and implement effective safety measures.
The top model developer team wins $250,000, with $100,000 for second place. The red team demonstrating the most effective vulnerability identification also wins $250,000, with $100,000 for second place.
Application proposals to participate are due Sept 1, 2024.For more information about the challenge, including rules and frequently asked questions, visit the [Amazon Trusted AI Challenge](
[URL:amazon.science]
"
[URL:amazon.science]
") landing
page.
You can also send email with questions to [
[EMAIL]
](mailto:
[EMAIL]
).
Best,
Michael Johnston
Amazon Artificial General Intelligence

subject: Announcement: an academic calendar for our future
body:
Announcement: an academic calendar for our future
Wednesday 16 April 2025
Dear students
We’d like to thank the thousands of students who participated in our consultation process to evolve our academic calendar – including more than 5000 respondents to the all-student survey, as well as those who took part in advisory and focus groups.
In the final round of consultation, two calendar options were identified for consideration – an optimised trimester model and a new semester model with a hexamester (six-term) overlay.
Your insights have been incredibly valuable in helping us understand the characteristics of an evolved calendar that best meets your needs and the merits of both models. You also helped us decide which calendar will accommodate the future needs of our University and align with the ambitions of our new
UNSW Strategy: Progress for All
.
In 2028 the University will adopt a
semester calendar with the flexibility
to
deliver differentiated learning experiences to our diverse student cohorts.
This
calendar – a ‘flex-semester’ calendar – centres on delivering the best learning experiences and outcomes for all our students throughout their lifelong education journey.
The choice in calendar also ref [ORDER_ID] the overwhelming view of those who participated in our consultation process – to have a semester calendar with the flexibility to support the diverse needs of all our students.
The flex-semester calendar is a semester structure for our undergraduate and the majority of postgraduate students alongside an aligned hexamester (six-term) structure to support the growth of lifelong learning opportunities, including for our alumni throughout their careers, and more flexible postgraduate offerings.
The key features of
our new calendar include:
two standard 12-week teaching semesters
a dedicated mid-semester break, more aligned to
other major university and school holidays
optional s
ummer and winter terms for intensive or catch-up courses and accelerated study
alignment with UNSW’s existing hexamester calendar
(UNSW already offers some programs in a hexamester (six-term) model. The new calendar enables us to expand these offerings to suit specific student groups, typically postgraduate learners and professionals looking to upskill).
Our flex-semester calendar reimagines how we deliver education and acknowledges that you, our students, have diverse needs and learn in different ways. It will:
give students more time with a 12-week semester, especially our undergraduate students who have told us they want to engage more deeply with course material
provide flexibility and choice around study load, learning mode and balancing other life commitments such as paid work and extra-curricular activities, and access to new learning opportunities across the academic year
expand flexible learning options – online and in-person – for postgraduates and working professionals, ensuring greater access to education for all
allow academics and course designers to adapt in the future to accommodate the changing needs of our student cohorts.
When will this change happen?
We aim to introduce our new flex-semester calendar in 2028.
This timing means the majority of our current students will not be impacted by the calendar change. For those students whose program will straddle both the current calendar and the new flex-semester calendar, the University will work to ensure minimal disruption to your studies and will work with you to resolve any issues with your study load and facilitate the completion of your degree program on time.
Thank you again for your support and contributions to this important evolution of our Academic Calendar. We will continue to keep you updated as we finalise calendar details and move towards implementation in 2028.
For further information and FAQs visit our Academic Calendar
website
and you can direct any
enquiries to our
Student Hub
.
Best wishes
Professor Attila Brungs
Vice-Chancellor and President
Professor Sarah Maddison
Deputy Vice-Chancellor Education & Student Experience

Lab Test 2 practice is now
Lab Test 2 practice is now
by
Dinh Tran
- Friday, 19 July 2024, 1:45 PM
Dear MATH1241 Students,
The Lab Test 2 practice materials are now available on Möbius and the test will be in Week 10 in the EXM class from your timetable on myUNSW. More detailed Lab Test 2 information is available on Moodle in the Lab Tests section of the Moodle page.
Like Lab Test 1, you have all of the questions in advance to practice and so you should be aiming for a high score. Unlike Lab Test 1, for Lab Test 2 you will be able to use Maple during the test and, in fact, some questions require the use of Maple. However, the majority of questions are designed to be solved by hand. In fact, many questions are easier to solve by hand and in some cases, significantly so.
If you would like the opportunity to take your Lab Test 2 on early, there will be some test sessions available at various times during Week 9. Please send an email to [EMAIL] and mention this announcement if you would like to take advantage of this.
Best regards,
Dinh Tran (MATH1241 course convenor for T2 2024)

Assignment submission links now available
Assignment submission links now available
by
Wolfgang Schief
- Tuesday, 26 March 2024, 12:07 PM
Dear Students,
The MATH1141 assignment information and questions were released in Week 5 and the deadline for your written submission is on Tuesday of Week 8. You should be well underway with this now. If not, don't delay in starting work on this.
Please read the instructions in Assignment section of the Moodle page carefully because the main focus of the assignment may not be what you are expecting.
Before you can submit your written work, you must complete a pre-submission checklist. This is now available in the Assignment section of the Moodle page. You are advised to complete this checklist now well ahead of the deadline because you may realise that
you need to revise your work as a result.
Very briefly... the questions are available on Möbius and you have a choice of flavours. You can try out all of the flavours on Möbius before choosing one. Although a small fraction of the marks is for some answers on Möbius, the main task is to type up
nicely explained answers and submit this to Turnitin on Moodle. This must be typed, not hand written and you will see in the marking rubric (which is provided in the assignment instructions), that the focus is on mathematical communication.
To help you with this, there are some mathematical writing resources in the Assignment section of the Moodle page.
Kind regards,
Wolfgang Schief
MATH1141 Course Authority

subject: Sturep nominations 2024!
body:
2024 Sturep nominations are open!
Dear CSE Student,
We are looking for nominations for our CSE Student Representatives “Stureps”. These representatives have the very important role of facilitating effective communication between our staff and students.
By participating on this committee, you have the opportunity to contribute to discussions and decision making for the School on academic and research matters. You will also gain valuable experience in advocacy, negotiation and teamwork (plus it looks good on
the CV!)
We have the following Sturep positions to fill for 2024:
CSE Undergraduate Stureps for 1st, 2nd, 3rd and 4th+ year – 2 per year
Postgraduate Coursework Stureps – 2 representatives
The CSE UG and PGC Stureps will be responsible for:
Complaints, questions and feedback from UG or PG and research students
Meeting with the Head of School and Executive to provide feedback
Maintaining the Stureps webpage
Providing updates at school meetings
How to nominate
Please email your nominations (using your UNSW email) to
[EMAIL]
by
5pm Wednesday, 5th June 2024 (1 week's time) with the following information:
Nominee’s Name
Nominee​’s Degree and progression
Reason why they believe they would be a good candidate (100 words), to be displayed on the election form so that
voters can attempt to make an informed decision
When nominating, please be aware of the following:
The nominees must be enrolled in a CSE Program (Degree) or a combined CSE Program
You can nominate yourself or another CSE student. If you are nominating another student, please ask them to
email
their
acceptance with the above information
by the close of nominations
Late nominations will not be accepted
Nominations after the election will not be accepted, even if not all roles are filled
We encourage you to consider nominating yourself or a peer and bring your experience, values and insight to the work of the Sturep committee.
Wishing you a fruitful year of studies,
- The School of Computer Science and Engineering team
--

location, date and time of your exams, and
a list of authorised materials
.
For more information, read our guide on
understanding your exam timetable
.
More support & info
Exam rules & Special Consideration
Before taking your exams, you must be aware of your student responsibilities under the
UNSW Exam Rules and Assessment Policy
.
Please read up on the
Special Consideration
process so that you are prepared for any unexpected issues.
⚠️ Student ID requirements
You need your physical UNSW student ID card
to sit your final exams on campus.
It will be scanned at the exam venue as part of a new attendance checking process. Electronic IDs will not be accepted.
Get your student ID card
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
MFA is a requirement
at UNSW and provides an additional layer of security to help protect the University and your account [ORDER_ID] unauthorised access.
Please ensure you have your authenticator smartphone fully charged and available on the day of your in-person or online exam to ensure you have access.
Library access
The UNSW
Main Library
,
Law Library
and
Paddington Library
are all available as study spaces, including some bookable individual and group spaces. If you need to
borrow a laptop
for your on-campus, in-person exam, you can check availability at the library.
Timetable clash
If you have an ‘all day’ exam that coincides with another assessment,
this is not considered a clash
, as you will have all day to complete the exam.
Supplementary exams
If you are approved for a supplementary exam, your myUNSW timetable will be updated no later than
Friday, 22 May 2026.
The supplementary exam period for Term 1 will be
Monday, 25 May to Friday, 29 May 2026.
Please regularly check your
UNSW email
and check the
supplementary exam page
for more information.
Exam adjustments
Equitable Learning Plans (ELP)
If you have an ELP that includes exam adjustments, please confirm these with your cours
e convenor or school.
The cut-off date for any changes to the adjustments in your ELP is
Wednesday, 22 April 2026.
P
lease contact
ELS
if you have questions.
If you still need to register with Equitable Learning Services (ELS) and require exam adjustments, please
book an appointment
as soon as possible.
Religious obligations
If your exam conflicts with your religious obligations, please complete a
Special Consideration
application and attach supporting documentation by
Wednesday, 22 April 2026.
Once your application is assessed, if approved, your timetable on myUNSW will be updated with any alternative arrangements.
Academic Misconduct
UNSW takes disciplinary action against students who engage in examination misconduct, including cheating or unauthorised use of notes, phones, or other equipment in exams.
The
Complaints Management and Investigations Policy & Procedure
sets out the penalties that may apply, including reduction in marks, failure in the course, fines, and suspension or exclusion from UNSW.
Back to top ^
Looking for help?
For advice on all things UNSW, including faculty specific questions, essential services and even support and development, reach out to the Hub!
Contact The Nucleus: Student Hub

by
Jonathan Kress
- Friday, 5 April 2024, 1:09 PM
Dear Students,
The Lab Test 2 practice materials are now available on Möbius and the test will be in Week 10 in the EXM class from your timetable on myUNSW.  More detailed Lab Test 2 information is available on Moodle in the Lab Tests section of the Moodle page.
Like Lab Test 1, you have all of the questions in advance to practice and so you should be aiming for a high score.  Unlike Lab Test 1, for Lab Test 2 you will be able to use Maple during the test and, in fact, some questions require the use of Maple.  However, the majority of questions are designed to be solved by hand.  In fact, many questions are easier to solve by hand and in some cases, significantly so.
If you would like the opportunity to take your Lab Test 2 on early, there will be some test sessions available at various times during Week 9.  Please send an email to [EMAIL] and mention this announcement if you would like to take advantage of this.

Jonathan Kress and Wolfgang Schief

body:
Hi everyone!
Welcome to COMP1531. If you haven't done so yet, come introduce yourself.
Our course websit...
‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌
Y
Yuchao Jiang
COMP1531 –
Announcements
😎 IMPORTANT - Pre-course information
Hi everyone!
Welcome to COMP1531. If you haven't done so yet, come
introduce yourself
.
Our course website is here
. You can see the
course outline
there too. We don't use moodle for this course.
There are just three things you need to do or be aware of this week.
🟡1. First Lecture
Our first lecture will be Monday the 27th of May from 11am-1pm in
Physics Theatre (K-K14-19)
. We will also stream the lecture online (
LINK HERE
).
🔵2. Group Preference Form‼️‼️
The form is due on Saturday 1st of June at 11pm!!
CLICK HERE TO FILL IT IN
.
As specified in the
course outline
, COMP1531 has a major project component that is a 5-person, 9-week group project that constitutes 90% of the course assessment. The successful and equitable formation of amazing groups of students for this major project is a priority for us. And we need to finalise these groups before week 1.
For us to form groups effectively, you
are required
to complete the
preference form
.
This form asks you to:
Optionally preference
only 1
other student to be in your group of 5.
If you preference someone, to avoid us disregarding the preference, they must:
(1) Be enrolled in the same class at the same time (i.e., same code on MyUNSW)
(2) ⚠️
Fill out the form preferencing you as well (we disregard preferences if it only goes one way)
⚠️
Answer a few further questions that help us form groups, regardless of whether you have a person-preference.
Here is a quick Q&A about the project and groups:
Q. Will you accept late preferences?
A. We sadly aren't in a position to accept late preferences, so please fill it in prior to then. Preferences are not guarantees that you will be in a group with that person, however, in nearly all cases we make it work.
Q. What if I don't have a preference?
A. If you don't have anyone to preference or only have one preference, don't even worry! That's very normal. You might hear murmurs from other students that you could end up with a "horror group" for COMP1531. Believe it or not, typically about 50% of student's don't provide preferences, and we just randomly match students in the same class.
Q. I'm scared about being in a bad group and them pulling my mark down, help?
A. We can assure you that while groups that struggle do exist, they only make up a small number of groups. We have structured the course and assessment
very intentionally
to have processes in place to ensure that if you work hard and follow the rules, your accomplishments should ref [ORDER_ID] your mark,
even if you end up with some challenging group situations.
In fact, in 24T2 we've devised the most individualized marking approach where essentially we have the capacity to mark any student individually from their group.
Q. Can I be with my friends even if they're in a full class I'm not in?
A. Lastly, a reminder that we can't manually enrol you in a full class, and we aren't accepting more than one preference per person as this ensures equitable group formation.
------------------------------------
We'll reach out again later this week with more information about getting started and first classes!
👋 Until then, enjoy your week can't wait to meet you all

subject: COMP3821-COMP[PHONE]_01545: Midterm Information
body:
COMP3821-COMP[PHONE]_01545
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
Midterm Information
H
Midterm Information
by
Liam Heng
- Thursday, 16 October 2025, 9:20 PM
This is a
long
post. Please read all the following information carefully.
Midterm Information
As stated on the course outline and the general course FAQs, the midterm is a 24-hour take home exam with the following timing:
Start Date: [Friday noon, Week 7] 31/10/2025 12:00 PM AEDT (UTC+11)
Due Date: [Saturday noon, Week 7] 01/11/2025 12:00 PM AEDT (UTC+11)
The midterm for this course is worth 20% of the final grade.
General information:
Assessable material is from Weeks 1-5. This covers foundations,
greedy algorithms (including matroids, SCCs, MSTs), intractable problems
(including reductions, up to and including approximation algorithms).
For further preparation, it is recommended that you attend Workshop
7, as some of the problems in that workshop will be on material that is
also assessed in the midterm.
You will be supplied with individual feedback on your work, along with a numeric mark assigned according to a rubric.
GenAI policy:
No Assistance.
That is, this assessment is designed for you to complete without the
use of any generative AI. You are not permitted to use any generative
AI tools, software or service to search for or generate information or
answers.
For more information on Generative AI and permitted use please see
here
.
If you use generative AI, you will be breaching the Student Code of
Conduct, and you are liable to disciplinary action under the Student
Misconduct Procedure.
Some updated/more specific advice; please see amended advice below:
The expected time commitment for the midterm is a total of 4-8 hours.
Original
[from: course information]: You are not
expected to spend the full period working on the exam (it is strongly
not recommended).
You should apply for Special Consideration if you are unable to commit a total of 8 hours due to an
eligible reason
, or if you find yourself unfit to take the exam.
Original
[from: course information]: You should
apply for Special Consideration if you are unable to take the exam for
the full period or if you find yourself unfit to take the exam.
How to sit the midterm
The paper will be made available on
MOODLE
at the start date listed above. It is here again, for ref [ORDER_ID]. [Friday noon, Week 7] 31/10/2025 12:00 PM AEDT (UTC+11).
Unlike
other submissions in this course, you need to make your submissions on
MOODLE
. The submission box is located below the exam paper.
You need to make a submission by the due date listed above. For
ref [ORDER_ID], it is [Saturday noon, Week 7] 01/11/2025 12:00 PM AEDT
(UTC+11). You can make as many submissions as you like, but only your
last submission is marked. We encourage you to submit early.
It is your responsibility to make sure you have submitted the right
files. No consideration will be granted if you submit the wrong file.
You should verify that your files are correct by downloading them after
submission.
If you do not make a submission before the due date, and you are not
awarded Special Consideration, any submissions you make are considered
late. In accordance with UNSW policy, the late penalty is 0 for any late
submission.
This is an exam. Therefore, there should be no communication with
other students. You should not be in the same room as another student
who is taking the exam. This also means that you should not be working
on your project during this time.
There may be reasons which make it difficult to comply with this
rule (e.g., sharing custody, being flatmates), and exceptions can be
made. You should inform by emailing the course account if this is the
case.
Please review the
exam misconduct rules
to ensure you do not fall afoul of the standards of the University while taking the exam.
You can handwrite and scan, or typeset your work (preferred). Your
work should be legible if handwritten; illegible work is liable to not
being marked accurately.
The forum will be read-only during this time. If you have queries please email the course account [ORDER_ID] materials:
This is an open book exam.
GenAI policy: see above.
Tentatively, there is an alternative exam scheduled for students who
are awarded Special Consideration. It is tentatively scheduled to start
on [Sunday noon, Week 8] 09/11/2025 12:00 PM AEDT (UTC+11).
How to prepare
A sample midterm will be released in due course with an accompanying
announcement matching the format of the exam. It is intended to
familiarise you with the format of the exam (There are five (5) problems
worth 20 marks each). The content assessed will be pulled from the
topics earlier in the announcement, however, the problems will not
necessarily be in the same order or quantity as the sample midterm as
the sample is indicative of format only.
You should review the previous workshop problems, as well as the
optional problems. Reviewing forum threads with existing discussion on
these problems may also be helpful. Opening new discussion is
encouraged.
You may also like to attend the help sessions. There are more staff
allocated to Week 6 than usual, so we hope that this session is used
appropriately.

Tutorials that fall on a public holiday
Tutorials that fall on a public holiday
by
Wolfgang Schief
- Friday, 15 March 2024, 7:57 PM
Dear Students,
As Friday of Week 7 and Monday of Week 8  are public holidays, tutorials that fall on these days will not take place.  If your tutorial is on the Monday or Friday, in the week that it is cancelled you can attend another tutorial, either in-person (board
tutorials and Q&A tutorials) or online (Q&A tutorials) or watch a recording of one of the online Q&A tutorials.
You can find the times and locations of other tutorials on the central timetable website:
[URL:timetable.unsw.edu.au]
Note that if you attend a different board tutorial in Week 7, you will need to tell the tutor the code of the tutorial that you usually attend.  This will be a code like M13A and you can find this in the text part of  your timetable on myUNSW.
Kind regards,
Wolfgang

body:

It's almost time to enrol in your 202
6

Hi Matthew,
Your 2026 annual enrolment appointment has been allocated.
This is the day and time from which you can
enrol in courses
for:
Term 1 202
6
Term 2 2026
Term 3 2026
It's also when you can course enrol and
class register for
Summer Term 2026
.
Where can I find my appointment?
Log in to
myUNSW
and look under
Enrolment Appointment
.
Appointments start from
Tuesday 7 October 2025
.
If you can't find your appointment time, please contact
The Nucleus: Student Hub
for help.
View your appointment
Frequently asked questions
What should I do if myUNSW is crashing or timing out?
While allocating enrolment times helps us improve the enrolment experience by reducing the traffic to myUNSW, there still could be lots of students accessing myUNSW at the same time when it's your turn to enrol.
If you are receiving errors, please try the following quick fixes before contacting
IT Services
:
Clear your cookies and cache
Open myUNSW in a private browser (incognito mode)
Log out of myUNSW and wait a few minutes before trying again
Why does my friend doing the same degree have a different time?
Your enrolment time is allocated based on multiple factors. Even a small difference between you and your classmate may mean you have been allocated a different time.
Your time cannot be changed
.
If you have Units of Credit from a different program or institution that you have not transferred to your current Program, please contact
The Nucleus: Student Hub
for help.
Choosing your courses
Course enrolment guide
Need help with your enrolment? Our course enrolment guide includes step-by-step information on how to enrol, as well as common troubleshooting tips!
Enrolment guide
How do I decide what course to enrol in?
Log in to myPlan
and search for courses and create your own study plan, or apply a recommended pathway based on your program and specialisation.
myPlan will tell you which courses are available to you each term. You can also check pre-requisites, map your progression and keep track of your program requirements.
Create your study plan now so that at the time of your enrolment appointment you can enrol easily and quickly in myUNSW, by selecting ‘Get courses from myPlan’ to pull them across to your enrolment basket.
Learn more about myPlan
What are some additional resources for course planning?
Use the
UNSW Handbook
to search for courses and detailed course information.
Refer to the
progression plan
(also called a sample program or degree template) for your program.
Use the
Class Search in myUNSW
to search for courses.
Note that students in their honours year still need to course enrol and class register for each Term.
When do I register for class times?
Remember, course enrolment is only step 1 of a 2 step
enrolment process
at UNSW.
You will still need to complete step 2 (
class registration
) and choose your class times before each term to confirm your enrolment.
You will receive a class registration appointment closer to the start of each Term, which is when you can register for your classes to secure your spot in the course.
Make sure you are aware of the
Key Dates
that apply to you!
Looking for help?
For advice on all things UNSW, including faculty specific questions, essential services, support and development, reach out to the Hub!
Contact The Nucleus: Student Hub

Would you prefer a tutorial class in Parramatta?
Would you prefer a tutorial class in Parramatta?
by
Jonathan Kress
- Thursday, 15 February 2024, 10:36 AM
Dear Students,
This term, as a trial, we are offering some tutorial classes in Parramatta on Wednesday afternoon.  We know that there are many students who will find Parramatta more convenient than Kensington.  If Parramatta is a convenient
location for you and you can arrange your schedule so that you don't need to be on the Kensington Campus on Wednesday, you might like to consider switching your tutorial class to one of those listed below.
Note that there are study spaces and there will also be a staff member available for consultation on Wednesday in Parramatta.
You can change your enrolment yourself on myUNSW until the end of Week 1.  From Week 2, it will still be possible to switch to the Parramatta classes, but you will need assistance.  If you have difficulty switching your
class and need some help, please contact either the Nucleus or [EMAIL].  Please include your student ID number in any communication.
Two tutorial classes have the Wednesday (board tutorial) class in room Para-[ADDRESS], Paramatta (Level 5).
M16F 4991: Mon 16 (w1-5,7,9-10, Griff M18); Wed 17 (w1-5,7-10, Para-501)
T14G 5020: Tue 14 (w1-5,7-10, OMB G31); Wed 15 (w1-5,7-10, Para-501)
Note that there are also some MATH1131 tutorial classes at the same location on Wednesday afternoon.  If you are enrolled in both MATH1081 and MATH1131 taking both classes at the Parramatta Campus may be particularly attractive.  The MATH1131 classes in
room Para-501 are:
W12G 5156: Wed 12 (w1-5,7-10, Para-501)
W14F 5168: Wed 14 (w1-5,7-10, Para-501)
Regards,
Jonathan Kress
Director of First-Year Mathematics and Statistics

Sasha Vassar
COMP1511 –
Announcements
The final countdown
Hi everyone!
I hope you are all having a good weekend and are ready and pumping for your last week of Term and the final week of COMP1511 Programming Fundamentals 🎉 (although also sad face) Well done on getting through this term of Uni (for lots of you - your very first term at UNSW!) - what an amazing feat and just a little bit to go before you get a well-earned break.
Last week was the final week where we covered any new material, and we continued to discuss linked lists, leak checks and boundaries (with Tammy!)!
This week, we will be focusing on exam revision - from the poll in the last lecture, we will be focusing on harder Linked List revision questions (Monday), harder Arrays questions and of course command line/strings with some more mixed questions (Wednesday). Just comment below if you want to cover anything else!
Lectures this week
Monday 11-1pm
Lecture 16: Exam revision: Harder Linked List/Arrays questions as hurdles (slides to be uploaded closer to lecture, I am still writing some questions)
Wednesday 4-6pm
Lecture 17: Command Line/Strings/Mixed bag of tricks
You will be able to find this week’s live code
here
.
myExperience Surveys
The
myExperience surveys
are out now and ready for you to complete. Please take a few minutes (we will give you some time in the tutorials) to fill these out. We are genuinely interested in your feedback, as it helps us to improve the course for the students that come after you. We love feedback and find it incredibly valuable in helping us make better changes. Please remember to keep your feedback
constructive
, so that we can act on it! myExperience is confidential, your identity is not included in reports. Results of the survey are not made available until your course results are released.
Week 11 Revision Classes
We will be running Week 11 Revision Classes - this is your chance to come to a lab time and work on some problems with the help of our tutors - they will also help by answering any questions and filling in any gaps to help prepare you for the exam. I strongly recommend that you attend one of these if you are not sure where to start studying for the exam etc.
Classes are running:
Monday 2-4pm (Online) - Anivridh and Valerie
Tuesday 11am-2pm (Morven Brown G3) - Anivridh and Valerie
Other than that, I hope you have a great last week of term, that your assignments are going smoothly in both 1511 and in your other subjects and you are feeling positive about the exams.
Cheers,
Sasha and the amazing 1511 team 🌟

subject: Engineering Research Forum
body:
Dear Qingbin
Join us for the Engineering Research Forum on
Wednesday, 26 June at 5:30pm at Engineers Australia in the CBD
.
Many of the most rewarding engineering careers involve research and innovation. A PhD can equip you with the skills and knowledge to lead complex projects, drive industry change, or launch your own venture.
Take your first step towards enhancing your career through a PhD in Engineering and hear from our expert panel as they share how research has shaped their careers across sectors like consulting, entrepreneurship, leadership, and diverse
industries.
Professor Lucy Marshall – Deputy Vice Chancellor (Community and Leadership), USYD
Dr Michael Eckert – Principal, Bilkert Consulting
Dr Tiberio Caetano – Chief Scientist, Gradient Institute
Dr Ann Na Cho – Lecturer and Horizon Fellow, School of Biomedical Engineering, USYD
You’ll also have the chance to ask questions during the Q&A and connect with speakers and fellow students over food and networking after the panel.
��
Register now

subject: FINAL REMINDER: 2024 UNSW SRC Voting Link
body:
你通常不会收到来自 [EMAIL] 的电子邮件。
了解为什么这一点很重要
Dear UNSW Student
This email provides you with your unique link to vote in the 2024 Arc Student Representative Council Election.
Poll open date: Tuesday 22 October 2024 05:00 AEDT
Poll close date: Friday 25 October 2024 22:00 AEDT
The sign-in link below lands on the Poll Menu, which lists all your eligible ballots.
1. Click on the below voting link. This will take you to the "Poll Menu" page, listing all the ballots (positions) that you are eligible to vote in.
2. Click on a position you wish to vote for. 3. To read each candidate’s statement, select ‘Click for bio’.
4. Voting is optional preferential. Click on the box next to your most preferred candidate. This will mark them as with a “1” for first preference.
5. Vote for additional candidates if you want to by clicking the box of as many as you want. You do NOT need to vote for all candidates.
6. Once you are satisfied with your order of preferences, click on “Proceed to vote confirm” button at the bottom of the page, then “submit vote”.
7. After you have submitted the vote it will move to the next eligible ballot.
8. If at any point you wish to return to the original page, click on the "Poll Menu" option on the top right of the menu bar. This will take you back to the list of all the positions/ballots for which you are eligible to vote.
9. You can log out from there and come back later if you want.
Women's Officer Voting
The current list of voters for the Women's Officer position for the SRC Elections consists only of students listed on the university enrolment records as Female.
Female Identifying students are also eligible, but must be manually included in the list of voters.
If you are female Identifying and wish to be included on the list of eligible voters for the Women's Officer position, please email the Returning Officer at
[EMAIL]
stating your zID and name and request to be added. Voting rights will be available to you for the Women's Officer position a few hours later.
Disability Officer Voting
The current list of voters for the Disability Officer position for the SRC Elections consists only of students listed on the university records as being registered with the UNSW Student Equity & Disabilities Unit.
Students with a disability who are not registered with this Unit are also eligible, but must be manually included in the list of voters.
If you are a student with a disability not registered with the Unit and wish to be included on the list of eligible voters for the Disability Officer position, please
email the Returning Officer at
[EMAIL]
stating your zID and name and request to be added. Voting rights will be available to you for that position a few hours later.
All requests received will be kept in strict confidence
SIGN-IN LINK:   To access the poll menu and begin voting click this link:
[URL:bigpulse.com]
If the above link does not appear to work copy and paste it into your browser.
Important Security Warning: Do not forward this email because it contains your personal auto-sign-in link.
[URL:bigpulse.com]
Philip Binns

subject: RE: SPAR 6 & 7 Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The SPAR programming contests series continues with two Sprint contests in the coming weeks.
SPAR 6 will be held from 5 pm to 6:45 pm on Wednesday July 24
th
at K14 LG18 Piano, LG19 Organ and LG20 Clavier labs.
SPAR 7 will be held from 5 pm to 6:45 pm on Wednesday July 31
th
at K14 LG18 Piano, LG19 Organ and LG20 Clavier labs.
These competitions are suitable for both beginners and students with competition experience. We encourage you to take part and get a taste of competitive programming.
These events are held in conjunction between CSE and CPMSoc. We recommend that you compete in person, as we will provide free pizza and discuss solutions after the conclusion of the contest. Online participation is also available for
those teams unable to attend on campus.
These contests are organised by the South Pacific Competitive Programming Association. You can view the calendar of events and subscribe for email updates on the
news page
.
You can find the contest rules at
[URL:sppcontests.org]
. Most notably, this is a team contest for up to three students from the same university or high school.
If you competed in an earlier SPAR contest, you should re-use the same login details. Teams with consistently strong performances throughout the year may receive certificates to reward their efforts.
Otherwise, please register your team of up to three students at
[URL:contest.sppcontests.org]
.
If you don’t have a team, you can register and compete as an individual, or you can make a team with other students on the day.
If you have any trouble registering, please contact me
by email
.
Unregistered students are still welcome to attend, and I’ll help you get registered and start on the contest.
If you don’t have a team, you can:
find a team on the
CPMSoc
Discord server,
make a team on the day
, or
compete as an individual.
I hope you choose to participate.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva
UNSW ICPC Coach

subject: UNSW systems outage: Update
body:
Systems Outage
Sunday 21 July 2024
UNSW is still working through the impact of the major IT outage related to third-party software platform CrowdStrike, which caused disruption globally on Friday.
Over the weekend, a cross-disciplinary IT team worked to test and recover all the University’s systems, servers and applications.
Extra staff were also deployed to check and repair every device in CATS rooms and teaching labs in our Sydney and Canberra campuses to ensure minimal disruption on Monday.
What have we done?
All AAA services and student labs have been checked and are back online.
All 1600 PC computers in teaching labs have been individually checked and repaired where necessary.
All CATS rooms have been checked and repaired where necessary.
All critical systems have been restored.
What is still impacted?
A large number of UNSW systems and applications have been affected by the CrowdStrike event. We are systematically working through each one to test and recover. This work will continue throughout the week, with student services being prioritised.
What do you need to do?
If you have any issues with technology at UNSW, please contact
UNSW IT Service Centre
.
The University remains cyber-secure however we ask that you be alert for any phishing scams during this period. UNSW or any related provider would never contact you to share credentials or send links to reset passwords.
Next steps
The scale of the outage caused by the CrowdStrike event has been significant.
While our IT team has done their utmost over the weekend to anticipate and rectify any technical issues, some staff and students may still be affected this week.
We thank you for your patience as we work through this.

subject: Term 3 Exams - Important Course Information
body:
Install SEB and take a practice test!
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
SEB important update ahead of Term 3 final exams
Hi Matthew,
You have enrolled in a course in Term 3 that is using the exam platform
Inspera
and will have an in-person (face-to-face)
invigilated exam
.
In-person attendance at the UNSW Kensington campus for your final exam is a mandatory requirement for this course.
You need to come to campus for your final exam
even if you are enrolled in an online class (such as a lecture or tutorial).
This means that to complete your final exam, you will need to
come to campus, bring your own laptop and install the correct version of the
Safe Exam Browser (SEB)
.
Preparing for your Inspera Face-to-Face Invigilated Exam
1. Prepare your laptop
Don't have one?
2. Get SEB
Download and install
3. Plan your trip
Get to your exam on time
Important update: Install the latest version of SEB
Please note:
even if you have used SEB for an on-campus invigilated final exam before, you may have an old/unsupported version.
SEB has recently been updated for both Mac and Windows laptops. Please ensure that you install
the latest UNSW compatible version of SEB
on your laptop
prior to your exam, as this will take some time.
Your UNSW final exam may not work if you download SEB from somewhere else.
Mac version
SEB version
All macOS
3.3.3
Microsoft Windows version
SEB version
Windows 11 24H2 or Windows 11
3.8.0
Windows 10 and older
3.5.0
Once you have installed the correct SEB version for your laptop,
make sure you take a practice test
through Inspera to learn the controls and be exam ready. You don't want to be adjusting settings and options during your final exam!
Take an SEB practice test
Important reminders for exam day
Exam day can be stressful and we want to make sure yours is as easy as possible, so please:
Install the correct SEB version on the laptop you will use for your final exam
before
exam day and take a practice test.
Check your exam location and start time in your
myUNSW Exam Timetable
. The start time of your exam will either be 9.45am or 1.45pm.
Students should be
ready to enter the exam room 15 minutes before these times
. Late arrivals are only allowed into the exam up until 10.30am and 2.30pm respectively.
Check Moodle for details of your exam, including what you need to bring.
Keep an eye on your email inbox. We will send you a reminder later in the term as the exam period gets closer.
Please note:
There is an extra 15 minutes added to your exam time to assist with technical issues.
Learn more
What if I can't attend my exam?
You must apply for
Special Consideration
if you know that you can't attend your exam during the Exam Period.
You can read more on
Supplementary Exams and the application process
online.
For more information on UNSW exam policy, rules and key dates, please contact
[EMAIL]
What is an invigilated exam?
An Invigilated exam means that there will be a UNSW staff member supervising on campus.
Learn more about invigilation
What is Inspera?
The digital assessment platform that UNSW has adopted to help improve your exam experience.
Learn more about Inspera
What is the Safe Exam Browser?
A secure, custom web browser that will let students take online exams without cyber security concerns.
Learn more about SEB
Back to top ^
Looking for help?
For advice on your Inspera exam, including SEB setup or getting a laptop, contact our Exams team using the link below, or read more about Inspera and SEB online.
Contact the UNSW Exams Team

subject: Student ID card required for final exams on campus
body:
Important update
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Make sure you have your student ID card ready for exams
Hi Matthew,
A reminder that to
sit your final exams on campus,
you will need to bring your p
hysical UNSW student ID.
It will be scanned at the exam venue as part of a new attendance checking process. Electronic IDs will not be accepted.
No physical ID card, no exam.
A reminder that it's a requirement of the
UNSW Student Code of Conduct and Values
that you always have your UNSW student ID card with you when you're on campus.
If you are unable to provide your physical UNSW student ID or physical photo identification from a Government body with an English component, you may not be able to sit your final exam.
Get your ID card
See exam rules
Central invigilated in-person, on-campus final exams:
If your timetable has a room location on campus, your exam is being centrally managed.
Your T1 provisional timetable will be available on myUNSW from Wed, 1 April and your final timetable from Tue, 7 April.
Before your exam
Familiarise yourself with our
exam rules page
, where you will find information, guides and resources.
If your exam is on Inspera, you should
prepare now
by downloading the correct version of Safe Exam Browser (SEB) to the device you are using for your exam and completing a practice Inspera test.
Check your Timetable on
myUNSW
carefully for the date, start time, location and information about what you can bring into the exam.
Central invigilated in-person exams will ONLY start at either 9.45am, 1.45pm or 5.45pm.
Look out for emails from [NAME], especially if you have ELS provisions.
More information
On the day of your exam
Ensure you have your UNSW student ID card and a pen/pencil.
The only other materials you will be allowed to bring into your exam will be on your timetable.
If your exam is on Inspera, bring your device (fully charged with SEB installed), along with the charging cable and your smartphone.
Access to power is not guaranteed.
Arrive at your exam location at least 30 minutes before your exam start time.
Looking for help?
If you have a question about your final exams, reach out to the Exams Team at
[EMAIL]
.

body:
COMP3821-COMP[PHONE]_01545
»
Forums
»

Term Project Group Formation
H
Term Project Group Formation
by
Liam Heng
- Thursday, 18 September 2025, 12:55 AM
Hi all
Around now is the time when you should start forming groups of 3 to 6 students for the term project. The project spec is now available on Moodle. Additional information will be supplied later regarding project mentors, their interests, and availabilities so that you can get the best out of your project. Project mentor consultations will begin in Week 2, and a timetable will be posted as soon as it is finalised. You may not have formed your group yet, but it is a good idea to go and speak to the mentors during Week 2, as they can help you with your ideas for a project, and drafting your proposal.
If you are looking for group members, you can post on the forum under the "Project (Public)". It is a good idea to:
Post your zID.
Your interests, and a potential project you'd like to pursue.
You should title the post with the topic of your project, so that prospective group members can investigate your proposition and ask to join your group.
You should form your groups as soon as possible. A form will be linked on Moodle when the mentor information is posted to record your groups. Additionally, you should co-ordinate with your group and preference the project mentors on the form, as you will have the same allocated project mentor for the whole term (not necessarily your first preference). A brief (not necessarily final) working project title should also be included. This is due by the end of Friday Week 2.
A reminder that the project proposal is due at the end of Friday Week 3, on Gradescope.

subject: Invitation to Join a Vertically Integrated Project (VIP) in 2026
body:
Dear Qingbin,
Happy New Year!
Based on your strong academic performance, we would like to invite you to apply for a Vertically Integrated Project (VIP) in 2026.
VIP is a hands-on, for credit program where students work over multiple terms on real-world engineering challenges alongside academics, industry partners and multidisciplinary teams. Through
this experience, students build technical depth and the professional skills employers value, including teamwork, large-scale problem-solving, leadership and communication, with many teams also collaborating externally, competing internationally or delivering
real-world outcomes.
A sample of projects currently recruiting includes:
🛡️
CyberShield
–
cyber security, ethical hacking, data & AI
💻
ServiceUNSW
–
UX, app development & digital accessibility
🔋
NextGEN
Energy Storage
– renewable energy & long-duration storage
📡
Connected
Health
– digital health, IoT & wearables
💜
Purple
House
– engineering for Indigenous-led healthcare
♻️🤖
R²:
Robo Re-Cycle
– robotics, AI & sustainability
🛸
Space
Power Systems
– IoT, energy storage, power electronics for space missions
These are just a few examples of the projects on offer. You can explore all projects and apply
👉
HERE
!
If you are keen to build experience that stands out to employers while contributing to meaningful engineering work, we encourage you to apply.
We look forward to receiving your application.

At UNSW, we are united in our commitment to a university community that is inclusive, respectful, and free from racism. We foster a culture of speaking up
across our campuses. Racism - whether overt or subtle - has no place here, and we do not tolerate it in any form. Our involvement in the Australian Human Rights Commission’s Racism@Uni survey ref [ORDER_ID] our dedication to listening, learning, and taking action.
We continue to raise awareness through education and dialogue, and we have robust support systems in place to assist affected students and staff. We are hopeful that the results of the AHRC study will help us to improve the experiences of our students and
staff.
You are invited to take part in the first national survey on racism in Australian universities.
“Racism harms students and staff in Australian universities. We need urgent action against racism, and your perspectives and lived experiences are central.
With your help, the Racism@Uni survey can provide vital data to inform recommendations for real change”
-
Race Discrimination Commissioner, Giridharan Sivaraman.
All staff and students at UNSW are invited to participate in the Racism@Uni Survey. The survey asks about experiences and impacts of interpersonal and institutional
racism. The Australian Human Rights Commission will use information from the survey to develop recommendations to the government and university sector to foster a safe, respectful, and inclusive environment for all university students and staff.
The Racism@Uni survey is anonymous, and your participation will be confidential.
If you complete the survey, you can choose to enter a prize draw to receive
one of fifty $50 gift vouchers
.
Please note: If you choose to enter the prize draw you will need to provide contact details. This will be kept confidential and separate to your anonymous
survey answers.
Here is your unique link to the survey and further information
Link
to the survey
By taking part in this survey, you will contribute to important national dialogue and work towards creating change. While your contribution would be highly
valued,
participation is voluntary
.
If you do not wish to take part in the Racism@Uni Survey, please disregard this email and further reminder emails.
The Racism@Uni Survey is being conducted by POLIS: Centre for Social Policy Research at the Australian National University on behalf of the Australian Human
Rights Commission. The survey findings will inform the Commission’s Racism@Uni: A National Study for Change Report.
You can visit the Australian Human Rights Commission
website
for more information about the Racism@Uni Survey.
If you become upset or distressed when completing the Racism@Uni Survey, support and wellbeing services are available
at
UNSW
or on the
Australian
Human Rights Commission website
.
The ethical aspects of this research have been approved by the Bellberry Limited Human Research Ethics Committee (2025/03414)and the Australian Institute
of Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander Studies (AIATSIS) Research Ethics Committee (REC-0541Howard-Wagner).
Kind regards,
The Racism@Uni Survey team

subject: IMC Programming Contest Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The South Pacific Competitive Programming Association is running an
individual programming contest
on 12
th
April 2026, thanks to the support of IMC. We hope you’re excited to apply your knowledge and skills in this competition and win cash prizes.
Participation:
This contest is open to any university or high school student. Participation is free.
Register via
TeamUP
.
Location:
Students must compete from an invigilated site.
Students at the UNSW site will use CSE lab computers. Do not bring your own device.
Lunch will be provided at the UNSW site.
Format:
The contest will run for three hours. Times are to be confirmed, but competitors should plan to be at campus from 11am to 4pm, for
a short practice session (to test systems), followed by lunch and the official contest, and finally a presentation.
The contest will consist of about four programming problems.
Progression and Prizes:
This contest has two stages. The qualifying round is held on 12
th
April, distributed across many sites in Australia and New Zealand. The top 20 students from across the region will be invited to the finals
at IMC’s Sydney offices on 10
th
July. Note that contestants must be at least 18 years of age at the time of the finals to be invited.
Cash prizes will be awarded to the top placed contestants at the finals. The prize pool is around $10,000 (to be confirmed).
Volunteering:
Student volunteers make these events possible. If you would like to help out on the day, please complete the
volunteering form
.
I hope you choose to participate or volunteer.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva
UNSW Competitive Programming Coach

The Mind Smart self-help guide from Psychology and Wellness has tips to help you manage it.
Check it out

Read on
.
In this week's Student News
get excited for
Diversity Festival
, sign a
petition
to help international students
and start planning your
exchange adventure
!
The Official Stuff
Planning on using a calculator in your exam? 🧮
Make sure it's got the 'UNSW Approved' sticker. You can pick up the label from The Nucleus: Student Hub.
Check eligible calculators
.
Get support at the GV Portal
If you experience gendered violence, get support at the GV Portal. You’ll be connected with a team of mental health clinicians who will listen and provide guidance.
Learn more
.
T2C enrolment and payment deadline 🚨
Sun, 24 Aug, 11.59pm
Sunday is the last day you can add or swap courses and pay your fees for T2C.
Learn more
.
Employability
Take part in Work Integrated Learning (WIL) 💭
Through our WIL programs and courses, you can work directly with industry and community partners for credit towards your degree.
See available opportunities
.
Opportunities
Apply for the Westpac Future Leaders Scholarship 💸
Apps close on Wed, 27 Aug, 5pm
Interested in postgraduate studies in 2026? You could get
$120,000
for coursework or research, along with networking and leadership development opportunities.
Explore the scholarship
.
Are you a maths whiz? Enter a comp to win prizes! 🧠
Regos close Fri, 29 Aug
We're looking for individuals and teams to enter the
Simon Marais Mathematics Competition
. A significant prize pool is up for grabs.
Sign up
with the passcode 'smmc2025UNSW'.
Submit your story to the Sounds of Home zine! 🎵
Submissions close on Mon, 1 Sep
We're seeking contributions to the Music Performance Unit’s first edition of the Sounds of Home zine, created for the 2025 UNSW Diversity Festival!
Submit now
.
Learn about startup entrepreneurship 🚀
Enrol in our elective subject
GENE1500:
Creative Entrepreneurship, to earn uni credits while you learn how to launch your own business or startup.
Learn more
.
From your Faculty
Your Faculty Noticeboard
See your faculty's latest news, opportunities and events.
Check it out
Engineering | 2026 RØDE Microphones Scholarship 🌟
Apps close Fri, 12 Sep
Receive financial support while advancing your career in mechanical engineering, electrical engineering or computer science! Four scholarships are available.
Apply today
.
Support
Student study tip of the week 💡
"If your brain feels tired or you just can't seem to tune in, do a body scan: when did I last drink water? when did I last eat something? When was the last time I got up and walked around? When did I last touch grass? If any of your answers are unsatisfactory, address those first before you knuckle down on studying."
- Urvee (B. Advanced Science (Hons))
Submit a tip
If we publish your tip in Student News this term, you'll get a
$5 voucher
to
The Little Marionette
. 💸
Get fast feedback on your writing 🖊️
Submit your essays, reports or reviews and receive personalised feedback on structure, grammar and ref [ORDER_ID]
within 24 hours! It’s free, UNSW-approved and available 24/7.
Submit now
.
Watch a Study Hacks recording ✅
Missed a study hacks workshop? Don't stress,
watch the recordings
.
Need help planning your exchange adventure? 🤔
We have online application assistance and drop-in advising sessions available.
Sign up
.
Events
Diversity Festival is back! 💖
Mon, 22 Sep until Fri, 26 Sep
With a vibrant, community-powered program of events, Diversity Festival 2025 celebrates the people, stories and ideas that make up our diverse UNSW community.
Explore the program
.
2025 Taste of Research Poster Showcase 🥽
Wed, 17 Sep, 11am - 2pm, Leighton Hall
Explore exciting hands-on projects and discover Engineering and Science research scholarships that could be yours!
Secure your spot
.
Arc Fun!
Check out the Food Hub 🍎
The Food Hub is a free food pantry on campus. Stop by to pick up nutritious food to fuel your studies. You must register in advance, plus don't forget to bring your containers!
Learn more
.
Looking for more Arc fun? 🫶
There are loads of events and activities taking place weekly on campus.
Check it out
Everything Else!
Congrats to the photo comp winners! 📸
We asked you to share your best pictures of reuse in action for the
Choose to Reuse challenge
, and you all delivered! Each winner will receive a $50 Grad Shop voucher.
See the winning photos
.
Sign the petition: fair fares for international students🚉
Did you know that NSW is still the only state without public transport concessions for international students? Let’s change that.
Sign today
.
And finally...
Feeling low? Let's talk
TalkCampus is an app that allows you to talk to other students around the world 24/7 if you're struggling, overwhelmed or just need a chat with someone who really understands what you're going through.
Download TalkCampus

subject: Free online career tools for UNSW students
body:
A wealth of free online tools, exclusive for you, to help enhance your career journey and boost your employability:
Graduates First
Ace the interview process with access to over 160 industry-standard job assessments and tools.
Sign up
Resume Checker
Enhance your resume with AI-powered feedback to help get the job you want.
Sign up
UNSWConnect
Explore over 3,000 active graduate, internship and part-time roles across Australia and in over 30 countries.
Sign up
Sign up to all three today!
Be a leader for sustainability
Offered in T2C, develop sustainable leadership skills in an interdisciplinary team via
CDEV3500
and earn 6 UOC. Learn how forward-thinking leaders are working to solve current real-world problems.
“I really enjoyed all the different presentations by industry leaders. I found the process of developing a solution and pitching to potential investors very useful.”
-  Clara, Bachelor of Bioinformatic Engineering & Science
Find
out more
T2 leadership training
Discover your unique leadership identity in Term 2.
Leadership Foundations
will equip you with professional skills that you can
apply in a diverse range of settings.
Best of all, the program is free!
Register
here
Health & Wellbeing Ambassadors
An AHEGS-recognised professional development opportunity for students passionate about promoting health and wellbeing at UNSW.
Learn
more
Find out more
about UNSW Advantage.
CDEV3300 Term 2C in Japan
Travel to Japan for 2-3 weeks and work on one of two interdisciplinary group projects in Tokyo, Hachioji or Narita. Applications close 3 April.
View trip details
The Big Meet - a big success
Exciting results! UNSW student registrations for
The Big Meet
career exhibition at the ICC in Darling Harbour more than doubled (128% increase to be exact) compared to 2023. Thank you to all who attended and engaged with Australia’s top industry representatives.

subject: Australian Government security and intelligence careers
body:
Opportunities in security and intelligence
Hi Qingbin,
Curious about a career in security and intelligence?
Don’t miss this unique on-campus information session with a Canberra-based Australian Government agency from the National Intelligence Community.
With their discreet global operations, this is a unique chance to get behind the scenes, ask questions, and explore your future in national security.
The presentation will appeal to students interested in:
world events and Australia’s relationship with other countries
international security and intelligence issues
seeking to make a difference through their employment
contributing professionally to the ‘bigger picture’ and Australia’s place in it.
Event details:
Date: Wednesday 24 September
Time: 5pm - 6.30pm
Venue: Colombo Theatre C, Colombo Building, UNSW Kensington
RSVP: Friday 19 September
Reserve your spot
Please note:
Registration is essential.
Due to the security requirements of this organisation, only Australian citizens are invited to attend.
No digital devices will be allowed in the room for the duration of the session. Please leave them at home or there will be a secure location to leave them on arrival.
Please do not discuss this invitation with others.

subject: MATH[PHONE]_00240: math1081 lecture notes
body:
MATH[PHONE]_00240
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
math1081 lecture notes
math1081 lecture notes
by
David Angell
- Friday, 16 February 2024, 5:42 PM
Dear students,
Lecture notes for MATH1081 topics 3 and 4 have been posted on Moodle.  To find them, open the "Lectures" tab on the Moodle page.  You will need to bring these along to lectures in week 4, either on paper or on a device.
Of course you are welcome to read them now if you want a sneak preview!
The notes are provided at full display size.  If you want hard copies, I recommend you save paper by printing 2 or 4 to each A4 page.
Best wishes,
David Angell

subject: MATH[PHONE]_00240: Lectures begin tomorrow!
body:
MATH[PHONE]_00240
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
Lectures begin tomorrow!
Lectures begin tomorrow!
by
Sean Gardiner
- Monday, 12 February 2024, 5:00 PM
Dear students,
We're looking forward to seeing many of you in the first live lecture tomorrow (4pm Tuesday 13 February, in the Physics Theatre)!
Nathan, David, and myself will give brief introductions before we launch into the course content. The lecture notes for Topic 1 are now available on the main MATH1081 Moodle page, under the "Lectures" section
here
.
It is strongly recommended to bring a (preferably digital) copy of the lecture slides to these lectures, so that you can annotate them as they are worked through. You will often find that exercise solutions and proofs have
been left blank for you to fill in as we go.
The live lectures will also be live-streamed via
Echo360
, and recordings will be made available soon after each stream has ended.
I hope you find the Discrete Mathematics course interesting, enjoyable, and rewarding!
Best regards,
Sean.

Peer Assisted Study Session (PASS) start this week
by
James Heath
- Monday, 19 February 2024, 1:57 PM
Dear MATH1081 students,
Peer Assisted Study Sessions
(PASS) start this week! Everyone is welcome to join these group study sessions
facilitated by friendly senior students who have successfully completed MATH1081
before.
Timetable
PASS Sessions run from Weeks
2-10. (Note: there are no sessions on public holidays or in Week 6.)
Time
Location
Leader
Monday 3-4pm
Old Main Building 230
Jake
Wednesday 1-2pm
H13 Lawrence West 3037
Jake & Leslie
Thursday 4-5pm
Online via Teams (click here to join)
Jake
Want a reminder?
Complete this
form
to receive a calendar invite.
Why should you go to PASS?
Past participants have said that
PASS provides a safe and welcoming environment where you can build confidence,
improve your grades and save time with smarter study methods. It’s also a great
place to meet people from your course and make friends.
Any questions?
Please contact
[EMAIL]
with any questions about
PASS. For more information, visit
student.unsw.edu.au/pass

subject: GENC[PHONE]_00696: Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
body:
GENC[PHONE]_00696
»
Forums
»
Announcements
»
Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
Reminder: Learning Community reminder – There are 3 things you need so submit
by
Andrew Hingston
- Wednesday, 11 March 2026, 10:42 AM
The
Learning Community Assignment
is
due
1pm
this
Thursday 12 Mar 2026
(Week
4
)
.
There
are
three
things that you need to submit:
1. Post a summary (and discussion of the practical applications) on an article
under the closest forum topic under the ‘Learning Community’ section of the
course website.
2. Provide the details of the article under the ‘Learning Community Details’
link under the ‘Learning Community Submission’ section of the course website.
This includes author, title, URL etc.
3. Copy and paste the text of your forum post AND a screenshot of the post into
the Learning Community MS Word Template and submit it under the ‘Learning
Community Report’ link.
The post (1) is for the benefit of your fellow students. Providing the details
(2) allows us to check whether the article that you chose is unique. Copying
and pasting your post into the MS Word template and submitting it (3) is what
is used for both plagiarism detection and grading.
Note that only doing step 1 doesn’t constitute submission. You need to do all
three!

Hi everyone,
Hopefully you have all been enjoying the Discrete Mathematics course so far, and are happy with your performance in the first lab test!

The first speaker is current PhD student Christian Bagshaw, whose name is no doubt familiar to many of you as one of the helpful MATH1081 Moodle forum moderators. He will be speaking about his research in Number Theory,

Dear students,
I firstly want to thank so many of you for submitting your feedback on our tutorials via the form on Moodle. Note that COMP3121/9101 is structured completely differently to COMP3821/9801. As of 25T3:
Instead of weekly workshop submissions, students submit a suite of
usually up to 4-6 problems a week, and resubmission is allowed.
Tasks are either complete or incomplete.
Workshops are replaced by labs, in which students are required to supply verbal demonstration of their submissions.
There is no midterm or project; the assessment is only the final exam and the portfolio (the aggregate of weekly tasks).
The cohort is significantly larger.

for the sake of brevity, but captures the main differences.
Nearly all positions in COMP3121/9101 will be for lab demonstration.
This involves a commitment of around 3h/week for marking, and a
commitment of 2h/week for a lab class.

COMP3821-COMP[PHONE]_01545
»

»

»
Nexus Lecture
H
Nexus Lecture
by
Liam Heng
- Thursday, 23 October 2025, 4:21 PM
Hi, I have asked to forward the following, as registration closes
tomorrow. This is for students interested in linear programming and the
running time of the Simplex algorithm (one of the top 10 algorithms of
the 20th century!). See below:
This is a small reminder of the upcoming Nexus public lecture, scheduled for next Tuesday at midday, followed by a light lunch.
Your presence is warmly welcomed.
For catering purposes, if possible, kindly register by this Friday.
Topic
The Maximum Diameter of Simplicial Complexes
Who
Professor Tibor Szabó from Freie Universität Berlin
When
Tue 28 Oct 12pm-3pm (lecture + light lunch)
Where
Anita B Lawrence Centre Kensington Campus (Rm 4082 / Rm 3082)
RSVP
Please register by Fri 24 Oct
Abstract
The Polynomial Hirsch Conjecture states that the diameter of the
edge-vertex graph of polytopes is bounded by a polynomial of the number
of their facets. This natural geometric problem is strongly connected to
the central algorithmic question on the worst case running time of the
simplex algorithm and its study motivated a number of abstractions and
generalizations. Santos in particular asked for the maximum diameter of
d-dimensional abstract simplicial complexes on n vertices. Following a
sequence of works by Santos, Criado, Newman, and Bohman, we determine
this maximum for every n when d=2, and for every large enough n when d ≥
3. The former construction is explicit, the latter one uses the
probabilistic combinatorial technique of iterative absorption. Based on
joint works with Stefan Glock, Olaf Parczyk, and Silas Rathke.
Bio
Tibor Szabó is Professor of Mathematics at Freie Universität Berlin,
working in extremal and probabilistic combinatorics. He received his
Ph.D. at The Ohio State University and has held positions at McGill
University, ETH Zürich, the University of Illinois, and the Institute
for Advanced Study. His research interests include extremal graph
theory, random and pseudorandom structures, and combinatorial games.
Schedule
12-1pm
Lecture in Room 4082, Anita B. Lawrence Centre, UNSW
1-3pm
Light lunch in Room 3082, Anita B. Lawrence Centre, UNSW

Wow … we are already up to

. We will be defining financial intelligence, modelling how much is required to achieve it and then taking an in-depth look at how the retirement savings system works in Australia.

(Tuesday 3 Mar 2026). The results and feedback will be available after the due date. Remember to ref [ORDER_ID] on your feedback and review the course notes for at least 24 hours before sending me any email queries. Please do NOT post queries about specific questions
in the General Forums.

Unit of the course, so I’d get this done sooner rather than later.

Financial Plan
each week as the course progresses. The idea is that you listen to the theory covered in the lecture, and then sit down and practically apply that theory to your situation and future goals
in your financial plan. By the end of this Unit, you should have written the first draft of the following sections of your financial plan:
1. Current Situation (Unit 1)
2. Life Planning (Unit 2)
3. Financial Strategy (Unit 2)
