Assignment: Peer Review Information and Week 6
by
Nathan Jackson
- Friday, 15 March 2024, 7:09 PM
Hi everyone,
I do apologise for a slight delay on the release of draft allocations for Stage 2 of your assignments. We had some technical issues with the allocation process. I'm pleased to confirm that all students who submitted their drafts on time have now
been assigned another student's draft to review.
If you submitted your draft on time, you will be able to visit the "Assignment draft submission" link - where you submitted your draft - to access the draft of one of your peers to assess. You will need to submit your review of this draft by 5pm
on the Wednesday of Week 7. Your peer review should be a PDF submitted to the Turnitin link under the "Stage 2" heading on Moodle, and you should use the provided Word template under the same heading to write your review.
If you missed the deadline for your draft submission, or have been granted an extension on the deadline, you can still submit a draft to the original link. You may have to wait up to three business days to be allocated a peer's draft. If you have
been granted an extension by Special Consideration or an Equitable Learning Plan, you will receive additional time to submit your draft and/or peer review. If you have not been granted an extension, standard late penalties apply as described in the assignment
information page and no additional time will be granted for the peer review. If no draft is submitted within 5 days of the draft submission deadline, you will not receive a peer's draft to review and hence will not be able to submit anything for Stage 2.
Your peer's feedback and a mark for the feedback that you submitted will be returned once all reviews have been marked. You should use this to help inform your final submission (which should fully answer all three assignment questions) due 5pm
Friday of Week 9. Note that peer review feedback may not be available until early in Week 9 and as such you are expected to continue working on your final submission between now and then.
If you believe that the assignment draft allocated for you to review is not a genuine attempt and/or cannot be sufficiently reviewed, you have until 9am Tuesday of Week 6 to fill out the report form found underneath the assignment link. You should
only do so if the draft allocated to you is very poor quality and cannot be reasonably marked with the provided rubric. If you do not submit a reallocation request by the deadline, or if your reallocation request is not approved, you will need to review the
draft you have been allocated.
I hope that you all have a fantastic weekend and week ahead. Do something fun and enjoyable over the break! As always, if you have any questions or concerns please do reach out.
Nathan

subject: Fwd: Bridging Theory & Practice: XRPL Hackathon Opportunity for UNSW Students
body:
If you are interested in blockchain or cryptocurrency, you may be interested in this hackathon being held at UNSW at the end of February.
I am advised that there will be a team formation activity at the start for people who don’t yet have a team.
Please see the enclosed Luma link for contact information if you have any further questions.
Ron van der Meyden
Professor,
School of Computer Science and Engineering,
UNSW Sydney
NSW 2052, Australia
email: [EMAIL]
web: [URL:cse.unsw.edu.au]
🚀 Opportunity: Build with Ripple’s Core Architects at the XRPL Hackathon
For those interested in the technical frontier of decentralized ledger, Ripple is hosting the XRPL Hackathon right here at UNSW.
This isn't your standard weekend sprint. Following the
XRP Australia
event, Ripple is bringing their global leadership and core protocol architects to campus to provide direct, hands-on mentorship and technical judging.
Why you should participate:
Direct Access:
Get 1:1 feedback from the engineers who actually design and maintain the XRP Ledger.
Global Competition:
Compete alongside international teams from NUS, NTU, and NKUST. Let’s show them the technical strength of the UNSW community.
Beyond the Prize Pool:
Ripple is using this event to scout talent for future XRPL funding programs and career pathways.
Registration:
Whether you are looking to break into blockchain or are an experienced dev wanting to stress-test your skills against the best, I highly encourage you to form a team and register.
XRPL Hackathon @ XRP Australia 2026 · Luma
luma.com

subject: Women in Nuclear Day – The Biggest Networking Event of the Year!
body:
Are you someone who is interested pursuing a nuclear career?
This is your chance to connect with industry leaders, top universities, and renowned companies at the
Women in Nuclear Day Event
- the biggest nuclear networking
event of the year!
Event Highlights:
University Opportunities:
Meet representatives from leading universities and companies including UNSW, ANU and University of Adelaide.
Industry Career Opportunities:
Connect with companies like Babcock Australasia, H&B Defence, KBR/Frazer Nash, and more.
Inspiring Speakers & Presentations
: Hear from top professionals and international speakers and gain valuable insights into the latest trends and
opportunities in the nuclear field.
Celebrate Women in Nuclear
: This one-day event, held on
November 7
, will also feature a special celebration of women in nuclear, honouring pioneers like
Marie Curie
and
Lise Meitner
.
Career Exploration
: Whether you're a student, graduate, professional, or just curious about the industry, you'll have the opportunity to learn about
exciting career pathways and potential collaborations.
Who Should Attend?
This event is open to all
students (both males and females), graduates, professionals, and anyone interested in the nuclear industry
. This full day event is the perfect opportunity for you to expand your network, gain valuable knowledge, and explore future career options in this
dynamic field.
Event Details:
Date:
November 7, 2024
Location:
UNSW Ritche Theatre
Cost:
FREE
Registration:
Click here to register now!
Don’t miss out
on this incredible opportunity to connect, learn, and grow in
the nuclear industry. Feel free to
share this event
within your schools, universities, and networks to help spread the word.
We look forward to seeing you there!
Kind regards
Barbara Vidos
Program Manager| Nuclear Innovation Centre
School of Mechanical and Manufacturing Engineering
Ainsworth Building
UNSW SYDNEY 2052

subject: Explore your career potential at UNSW
body:
Enhance your potential at UNSW through personalised guidance from a dedicated career coach via our
Career Explorer Program
.
This is your opportunity to embark on a transformative roadmap to employability, expanding your possibilities and unlocking countless future opportunities in an evolving world.
The program consists of three in-person or online (your choice) coaching sessions:
Session One – group session (45 mins)
Session Two – individual session (30 mins)
Session Three – individual session (30 mins)
Please note
: if you have completed the Career Discovery Coaching Program you will not need to do the
Career Explorer Program
, as it is the same program with a new title.
Initial group session times are now available for Term 2.

The fun stuff
Welcome back to Term 2, engineers! 🔧📚
We hope you had a relaxing and well-deserved break after a big Term 1.
Whether you spent it travelling, working or just catching up on sleep, it’s great to have you back on campus and ready to kick off a brand new term!
Term 2 is already buzzing with energy — and so are the Engineering societies, with heaps of exciting events, opportunities and programs coming your way. From industry nights and career panels to social mixers and tech workshops, there’s something for everyone this term!
Scroll down to see what’s happening and how you can be a part of it!
Trivia Time
Which iconic UNSW engineering invention helped revolutionise the modern Wi-Fi technology we use today?
A) MIMO antenna arrays
B) Wi-Fi signal encryption protocol

Discover the answer at the end of the newsletter!

A team of engineers at UNSW, working with the CSIRO, helped develop the WLAN high-speed radio chip that forms the foundation of today’s Wi-Fi. Their work enabled fast and reliable wireless communication, making streaming, online gaming, and remote work possible around the globe! 📶🌐💡

Get vaccinated for Hepatitis on campus 💉
Hepatitis is a blood-borne virus that can cause liver inflammation and some people are more at risk than others. Learn where to get your Hepatitis A and B immunisations on campus.
See more
.
Beware of phishing scams 🎣
Phishing emails use urgency to scare you into sharing personal information. Stay safe by checking that URLs use official domains, reporting dodgy messages as phishing and deleting suspicious messages.
Learn more
.
Protect your vehicles and belongings 🚨
There's been a recent increase in both car and motorcycle thefts. Ensure that your vehicles are insured and registered, always lock them and avoid leaving valuables inside. Report any suspicious behaviour to UNSW Security.
Learn more
.
We're improving the student experience at UNSW 💛
The Student Experience Program is a University-wide initiative created for you, based on what you’ve told us matters most! We’re working closely with students who are sharing their feedback and ideas to help shape the program and create meaningful change.
We’re working towards:
Better digital tools and platforms that make it easier to manage your studies.
Smarter assessments and feedback that help you learn and grow.
More moments for connection with peers, faculty and the wider UNSW community.
Simpler degree programs.

Student study tip of the week 💡
"Use the ARC term planner (or create your own) to write down all key dates for the term—such as assignment deadlines, exams, and events—and keep it somewhere visible. This helps you stay on top of upcoming tasks and plan ahead. Pair this with the ARC weekly planner to break down your goals into daily tasks.
The limited space on the weekly planner prevents overloading your schedule, helping you stay realistic and avoid overwhelm. It’s also motivating to tick off completed tasks and visually track your progress.
Extra Tip: Review your planner at the start of each week to adjust your plan based on new priorities or unexpected changes."
- Prisha (Bachelor of Software Engineering)
Submit a tip

Majoring in stress? 😩
We partner with TalkCampus, an online student wellbeing app. It's your space to anonymously vent, get advice or share the tea on what's going on in your world with other students.
Check it out

Counsellors share their wellness tips ❤️‍🩹
We asked counsellors from
Psychology and Wellness
what their top wellness tips are. Here's what they said:
“Breathe intentionally - take a few minutes each day to do deep breathing - inhale for 4 seconds, hold for 4 seconds and exhale for 4. It calms the nervous system quickly.”
“Intentionally unplug and step away from your screens, especially social media, even if it’s just 1 hour before you sleep.”
“I love to get out into the sun. It’s a natural mood booster.”

Every Tue, Wed and Thu, 9.30am - 12.30pm
Mornings can be tough, but a free breakfast can help. Find Brekkie Club by the Basser Steps and set yourself up for success with a dose of mindful ref [ORDER_ID].
Find out more
.
Looking for more Arc fun? 🫶
There are loads of events and activities taking place weekly on campus.
Check it out
Choose to Reuse
Forgot your reusables? Here’s how to still cut waste on campus ☕
Left your cup or container at home? Don’t worry, there are still smart, sustainable choices you can make! Try dining in
using reusable crockery at campus cafés or go lid-free to reduce single-use waste.
These small swaps help cut down on packaging waste and limit exposure to chemicals found in disposable containers.
Did you know? If you lined up all the disposable cups used at UNSW in a year, they’d cover the entire Library Lawn! 😱
Explore the Choose to Reuse Challenge
Sustainability tips of the week 💡
"Instead of wrapping gifts in wrapping paper, which gets thrown out straight away, I like to get creative and wrap gifts in nice fabrics or scarves that the recipient can reuse to reduce waste from gift wrapping (and it becomes a fun second gift!)"
- Nomi (Bachelor of Engineering/Science)
"Keep all clean plastic bags (especially from delivery) and reuse them as trash liners instead of buying new ones."
- Luthfia (Master of Education)
Submit a tip
If we publish your tip in Student News this term, you'll get a
$5 voucher
to
The Little Marionette
. 💸
There's still time to enter the BYO photo comp
🌱
We’re in the last weeks of the campaign, so keep those BYO cup and container photos coming! You’re not just in the running for prizes, you’re helping make UNSW more sustainable with every post.
Enter now
.
Everything Else!
Skip the food and drink queues! ⏩
Craving a hot brew or snack on campus but don't want to wait in line? Order [ORDER_ID] from
XS Espresso
,
Guzman y Gomez
,
The Little Marionette
and
Plume
.
Free period products! 🤩
Did you know that you can access free period products on campus at UNSW Kensington? Check out the updated period product dispenser map to find your nearest location.
Check it out
.
And finally...
Don't fall for the trap of contract cheating! ⚠️
Contract cheating is submitting someone else's work as your own. It's a big deal and has serious consequences. If you're struggling academically, there is plenty of support available.
Avoid contract cheating

subject: Faculty of Engineering - Best and Brightest Networking Event, Wednesday 11 June
body:
Dear Qingbin
The Faculty of Engineering invites you to attend the upcoming
Best and Brightest Information & Networking Event
as an aspiring guest.  This event will a celebration for the Best & Brightest students in the Faculty of Engineering who are currently undertaking their final year undergraduate program.
At this event, you will:
Discover the UNSW Engineering spinout,
Canopus Networks,
featuring
Professor Vijay Sivaraman
as our keynote panellist.
Founded in 2018, Canopus Networks emerged from UNSW research in Software Defined Networking (SDN) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies.
With over $18M raised in VC funding, the company has developed an AI Network Observability Platform that is currently operational in large Tier-1 Telecom Networks serving millions of fixed-line and mobile subscribers.
Canopus Networks continues to leverage PhD research at UNSW Sydney to maintain its Intellectual Property lead in the market.
Professor Sivaraman will be joined on the panel by key members of the Canopus Networks team and UNSW PhD candidates.
They will provide insights into the journey from academic research to commercial application, discussing the challenges and successes in developing cutting-edge network analytics and Agentic AI solutions.
Following the event, you are welcome to enjoy canapes and drinks, and connect with the Best and Brightest scholarship recipients and Faculty members to learn more about postgraduate research in Engineering.
Please refer to the below event details and secure your ticket [ORDER_ID] details
Date: Wednesday, 11 June 2025
Time: 4:30 – 6:30pm (registration from 4:00pm)
Location: Leighton Hall, John Niland Scientia Building
Registration link:
BEST and BRIGHTEST 2025 Tickets | Eventbrite
Thank you, and we look forward to seeing you at the event!
Regards,
A/Prof Vidhyasaharan Sethu
Associate Dean (Research Training), Faculty of Engineering

subject: Free resources for your recruitment process
body:
Time to celebrate!
Today officially marks the end of Term 3 exams and the start of summer break.
Thank you for an incredible year! 2024 has been amazing. We've seen a huge rise in enrolments across all our programs, courses and events this year, showing your progress to both academic and employability success.
Let’s keep striving to maximise your unique potential in 2025!
[NAME]
Three-step recruitment process
We offer FREE resources to support your three-step job search and application process this summer. Start your journey today:
1. Look for a job
UNSWConnect: Access thousands of graduate, internships, and part-time roles in Australia and around the world.
Find jobs
2. Resume preparation
Resume Checker: AI-based feedback on your resume to ensure it is ready to perform and get you interview callbacks.
Upload resume
3. Practice assessments
Graduates First: Free unlimited access to 160+ practice job assessments including aptitude tests, games and video interviews.
Get practicing
New year’s resolution: develop a new skill
In 2024, we saw a remarkable 46% increase in participation in our Employability programs!
Apply now for one or more of our FREE signature programs available in Term 1, 2025. These programs are designed to align your personal strengths with essential professional development skills:
Career Discovery Mentoring Program
find out more

find out more
Leadership Foundations
find out more
Professional Development Program
find out more
Four critical communication skills
Grades alone won’t get you your dream career. Knowing how to connect in the Australian job market, particularly as an international student, has never been more important. One of the most crucial steps to building your confidence is developing your communication skills.
Here are four tips to help you overcome cultural and language barriers and start a successful career in Australia.

subject: International Student Employability Showcase - register now!
body:
Dear Qingbin,
Are you ready to become a leader or changemaker who makes a positive impact on society, the environment, and people’s lives?
Join us at [NAME]'s Careers with Impact event and explore career pathways that truly make a difference. Connect with inspiring professionals from diverse sectors and organisations who are driving positive change.
Whether you're exploring options or ready to take the next step, this event is your gateway to a meaningful and impactful future.
📅 Wednesday, 1 October
🕒 From 3:00pm
📍 John Niland Scientia Building, Kensington Campus
Choose one or both sessions:
Careers with Impact: Shape the future you want
3pm-4:30pm -
Apply here
Meet the Employers and Changemakers Showcase
4:30pm-6:00pm
-
Register here
Gain tools, resources, connections, and insights into what an impact career looks like, and how to find one.
We look forward to seeing you there!
1. Careers with Impact: Shape the future you want
Apply to attend this session to hear from industry professionals across diverse impact roles and organisations and take part in group activities that will build skills to find a career that matters to you.
Highlights:
Keynote speaker Michael Rodrigues, Commissioner, Office of the 24-Hour Economy.
Finding your purpose session run by a [NAME] Coach.
Panel discussion and Q&A hosted by Professor Danielle Logue, Director, Centre for Social Impact UNSW.
Please note:
Places are limited and by application. Apply now to avoid missing out.
Apply Session 1
2. Meet the Employers and Changemakers Showcase
Open to all students that want to discover organisations, roles and pathways to make an impact in their career.
Showcase highlights:
Meet over 20 impact-driven organisations that are creating positive change.
Engage with representatives and staff that can share information on impact roles, initiatives and pathways.
Fun activities including build the future you want with
Lego Serious Play
, spark delight in your life with
(Daily) Delight~Disrupt
, and take home your own one-of-a-kind creation at the
Reverse Garbage Creator Club
.
Native plant and seed giveaways and information from
IndigiGrow
.
Enjoy free snacks, drinks and afternoon tea from
OzHarvest
.
Register Session 2

body:
EVENTS
📆
5 FEB AT 10:00 AM – 8 FEB AT 3:00 PM
📍
UNSW QUADRANGLE
CSESoc at O-Week
Time flies ✈️ when you’re having fun! As our holidays quickly come to a close with O-week approaching, your favourite computer science society 😉 will be at O-Week! We’re here to answer any questions❓ related to our society - whether this be events, workshops, subcom life or even opportunities 😲.
AND the cherry on top? 🍒
We’re releasing a bunch of CUSTOM merchandise of our beloved mascot Ollie 🦦, including stickers and even BUCKET HATS 🪣🎩

31 JAN AT 12:00 AM – 11 FEB AT 11:30 PM

ENGINEERING RD KENSINGTON, NSW, AUSTRALIA, NEW SOUTH WALES 2032
CSESoc 2024 Peer Mentee Registrations
🏷️ Attention all first-year undergraduates and postgraduates! The Computer Science and Engineering Society (CSESoc) is thrilled to announce the kickoff of our Peer Mentee Registrations for 2024. Benefit from the wisdom of experienced mentors 🧠, adjust to university life seamlessly 🌐, and for those grappling with COMP1511, fear not 💀, help is on the way! Let's learn 📚, grow 🌱, and conquer together. 🚀
Register here

1 MAR AT 12:00 AM – 3 MAR AT 3:00 AM
📍
ENGINEERING RD, KENSINGTON, NSW, AUSTRALIA
UNIHACK 10th Anniversary Hackathon
Get ready for the most electrifying hackathon experience ever as Atlassian and UniHack come together to celebrate their 10th anniversary! Spanning 48 hours over 3 days, attendees will have the unique opportunity to connect with mentors both on-site and online, ensuring a wealth of guidance and expertise throughout their hackathon journey. 🤝💻 Transcending from its traditional Melbourne roots, this year marks the event's first-ever SYDNEY HUB, making it HUGEE! 🌐🎉

to be notified of the event as soon as possible 🤩!

1 MAR AT 12:00 PM – 3 MAR AT 5:00 PM

JUNE GRIFFITH M17

Nearly there, engineers! 🎓
As Term 3 draws to a close, we hope you’ve powered through your projects and are ready to finish the year strong. Whether you’re preparing for exams or counting down to a well‑earned break, now’s the time to ref [ORDER_ID] on your wins, set fresh goals and celebrate how far you’ve come.
We’ve got an exciting wrap‑up of events, opportunities and end‑of‑year highlights to help you make the most of the final stretch. Keep reading to stay on top of what’s coming! 🚀
Trivia time
Which UNSW engineering breakthrough led to major advances in water recycling technology?
A) Biofiltration membranes
B) Reverse osmosis desalination
C) Smart sensing pipelines
D) Microbial fuel cells
Discover the answer at the end of the newsletter!
Events
Build a Boba 🧋
Craft your dream drink, sip your feelings and forget about your Week 8 to-do list! Build your free boba and customise it however your heart desires.
Choose from our jasmine/matcha milk teas or lychee oolong/peach oolong fruit teas and stack it with pearls, lychee or rainbow jelly, plus all the toppings you'll ever need.

2 - 4pm
Quad, Globe Lawn, Main Walkway
Sign up
CEUS x POKERSOC Games Night 🃏
Relax and unwind with some friendly competition and snacks. No money is involved and everyone’s welcome at the table!
If poker’s not your strong suit, don’t worry — there’ll be plenty of UNO, Catan and other classics to keep the good times rolling.
Thursday, 6 November
6 - 9.30pm
Mathews 320
Register now
Decode the Dots
🖐️
Open your mind and warm up those fingers, because this braille workshop is all about touching dots and feeling a connection!
We're teaming up with VisAbility, a WA-based organisation that provides support services to the blind and low vision community. Break down braille basics and connect the dots to truly understand what it’s like living with little to no vision.
Friday, 7 November
3 - 5pm
Ruper Myers 410
RSVP today
WIESoc x REDBACK Racing Hyper Karting 🏎️
Feeling restless after a long week of studying? Hyper Karting is the perfect fix!
Gather your mates for an adrenaline-pumping race featuring sharp turns, high-speed straights and unforgettable moments. Whether you’re a seasoned racer or a first-time driver, we guarantee laughs, chaos and high-speed fun.

subject: 📅 Exam info, a fresh UNSW job board and a beach safety course!
body:
Student News | T3 W7 📬 Here’s what’s happening this week at UNSW
For a better experience,
view your news online
. 🖥️
P.S.
Follow us on Instagram

The Arc International Markets are on this Wednesday!
🌏
Come down to Main Walkway from 4 to 9pm to celebrate cultural diversity through food and music!
Savour dishes from around the world, prepared by your fellow students. From Italian pasta, Japanese sushi, Mexican tacos to Indian curries, there's something for everyone.
View food menus
In this week's Student News, check out our new
job board
, learn
beach safety essentials
and join a conversation about
modern masculinity
.

Gadigal (City and Paddington campuses) and Ngunnawal peoples (UNSW Canberra).

Wed, 29 Oct

.
What is plagiarism? 🤥
Plagiarism happens when you use someone’s work and claim it as your own. You can accidentally self-plagiarise if you reuse your own work without citing it! Explore common forms of plagiarism and how to avoid them in your studies.
Read more
.
Stay connected and protected on campus 📲
The SafeZone app gives you a direct line to UNSW Protective Services. Get quick help in emergencies, request first aid or check in when studying late.
Download
.
Need help with off-campus accommodation? 🏠
Our OCAS Advisors are here to help with housing concerns, connect you with local services and more.
Book an appointment

Mental Health Month: It's Movement Week 🤸‍♀️
This is your reminder to get your body moving! Consider a 10-minute walk, a dance class or even a run. Plus, enjoy a seven day membership for
$7
at the UNSW Fitness and Aquatic Centre.
Learn more

Share your thoughts on major upgrades on campus 🏗️
We're proposing two major upgrades on Barker Street, including new student accommodation and a parking station upgrade. Join an engagement session to share your feedback before the proposals are submitted in Jan 2026.
Learn more

Be aware of lane closures and detours on Wansey Road 🚧
Night works continue until Fri, 28 Nov
Traffic light upgrades and the addition of a new footpath are happening at the Wansey Road and High Street intersection to improve safety. Please follow all safety signs during this time.

GV Portal

Book now

subject: FW: IMC Programming Contest Registration Announcement
body:
Dear students,
The South Pacific Competitive Programming Association is launching a new
individual programming contest
on 15
th
February 2025, thanks to the support of IMC Trading. We hope you’re excited to apply the knowledge and skills you gained in 2024 to this competition.
Participation:
This contest is open to any university or high school student (as of 2025). Participation is free.
Register using
this form
.
Location:
Students must compete from an invigilated site, such as UNSW CSE’s computer labs.
Students will use CSE lab computers. Do not bring your own device.
Lunch will be provided at the UNSW site.
Format:
The contest will run for three hours. Times are to be confirmed, but competitors should plan to be at campus from 11am to 3pm, for a short practice
session (to test systems) followed by the official contest.
The contest will consist of about three programming problems. There will be a sample contest made available in early February to familiarise yourself
with the systems and style of problems.
Progression and Prizes:
This contest has two stages. The qualifying round is held on 15
th
February, distributed across many sites in Australia and New Zealand. The top 20 students from across the region will be invited to the finals
at IMC’s Sydney offices on 11
th
May. Top performers in the qualifying round (more than just the 20 qualifiers) will also receive a T-shirt.
Volunteering:
Student volunteers make these events possible. If you would like to help out on the day, please complete the
volunteering form
.
I hope you choose to participate or volunteer.
Best of luck,
Raveen de Silva
UNSW Competitive Programming Coach

subject: [COMP2511] Notice: End of Week 8 Updates
body:
Sai Nair created a notice COMP2511 on Sun 03 November 2024 at 11:34:18 PM.
End of Week 8 Updates
Hey everyone, we've got a fair few announcements this week!
Assignment III 🎏
Assignment III has now been released, with both the spec and the repo available to you (and can be found on the
assessments tab
on [NAME]). Your groups are the same as in assignment II unless you have indicated that you would like a change, or if your assignment II group was a group of 3. To see who exactly is in your group, you can check the members of your repo. If you'd like to change your groups or do it solo, contact your tutor ASAP and they can change your group for you.
The assignment is worth
8% bonus marks
(which can only catch up on marks lost in other assignments, not labs or the exam), so not completing the assignment won't lose you any marks. It is due
Week 10 Friday at 5pm Sydney time, though no penalty is applied until Week 11 Tuesday at 5pm
. Submissions won't be accepted after 7 days past the
original deadline
i.e.
after Week 11 Friday at 5pm.
We will also be briefly going over the assignment spec in tomorrow's lecture, so stay tuned for that!
Sample Exam 🧑💻
In the week 10 lab, we'll be running a sample exam for you to gain experience with the exam environment. This is not worth any marks, but it is useful to gain familiarity with the system so you spend less time in the exam acclimatising and more time working on the exam. It will also enable you to look at some questions which will mimic the
type
of questions you'll see in the actual exam (the number and difficulty of questions may differ).
Note that the environment is only present on the CSE computers, so if you are in an online tutorial, you will want to attend an in person lab for week 10 - any such tutorial is fine.
Assignment II ⚔️
Assignment II is due
this Friday at 5pm
. A reminder that
no late submissions are accepted for this assignment under any circumstances
. If you have ELS provision which apply to the assignment, email the class account and we'll discuss other arrangements.
And, as always, feel free to hop in a help session or ask questions on the forum - we've got plenty of support in both regards!
Assignment I Reruns 🏃
A gentle reminder that the rerun form for assignment I will close this Wednesday at 11:59pm. All requests will be run shortly after.
Lots coming up these next couple of weeks - knuckle down for a couple more weeks, and we'll see you on the other side 🫡
You can view and reply to the message by
clicking here.
Reply to this email to contact Sai Nair (
[EMAIL]
).

body:
Dipankar Srirag 📸 updated a notice COMP6713 on Sat 14 February 2026 at 07:43:33 PM.
Welcome to COMP6713: Natural Language Processing (T1 2026)
Dear all,
Welcome to
COMP6713: Natural Language Processing
in 2026 Term 1!
Over the next ten weeks, we will explore and understand how computers process human (natural) language. The COMP6713 team is led by lecturer-in-charge:
Aditya Joshi
, with the help of course administrator
Dipankar Srirag
(myself), and tutors:
Austin, Amrita, Freya, Liangji, Martin, Mingqin, Rahul
.
Some quick reminders:
This is a hybrid course (in-person/online) with weekly lectures and tutorials. Lecture recordings will be available on
Echo360
.
You will be required to also participate in weekly quizzes, in addition to other assessments.
The WebCMS portal contains lecture slides and tutorial material. Material for Week 1 has been added to
WebCMS
, and will continue to be uploaded over the weekend in subsequent weeks.
The course will use
WebCMS forum
as a place to ask questions of the course team and also to discuss lecture/tutorial content.
We will not be monitoring other student forums.
There will be no tutorial in Week 1.
For any other queries, please reach out to the course email:
[EMAIL]
Our first lecture is on
Monday, 16th February, 2026, at 4:00pm
in
Mathews Theatre B
. See you there!
Regards,
Dipankar Srirag

subject: [COMP9517] Notice: Week 10 Planning
body:
Erik Meijering created a notice COMP9517 on Mon 04 August 2025 at 05:27:09 PM.
Week 10 Planning
Dear Students,
We have come to the final lecture week of this term, and no doubt you are all very busy completing the group project. Just informing you about the planning for this week:
Guest Lectures (Online):
Since we have already covered a lot of topics in the past weeks, we have decided to teach no new examinable material this week.
We have made several
guest lectures available on Echo360
, given by colleagues in the school and collaborators, showcasing advanced applications of computer vision. These lectures have already been recorded and you can watch them at a time that suits you, or you can use the Wednesday and Thursday lecture
time slots for that.
Lecturer Consultation Session (In Person):
So there will be
no lectures this week
other than the online guest lectures. However, if you have any remaining questions about the lectures or the project or the exam, I will be available in person in the lecture room on Thursday 4-6pm (not on Wednesday) to discuss.
Feel free to make use of this consultation session.
Group Project Submission:
The
submission deadline of the group project deliverables
is this Friday 6pm Sydney time. If possible, it is best to submit a bit earlier. Especially when many students try to submit at the same time, the system may struggle. See also the earlier post today regarding project submission and using the command
line approach rather than [NAME].
Exam
Questions & Answers:
On Friday evening I will post a more extensive list of
sample exam questions with answers
on [NAME]. If you have any further questions about these, do post them in the forum and I will follow up there.
All best wishes for this week!
Erik

body:
Good morning everyone!
Hope you are all having a great weekend, and are revving to get back into things f...
‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌
Sasha Vassar
COMP1511 –
Announcements
We are almost there :)
Good morning everyone!
Hope you are all having a great weekend, and are revving to get back into things for Week 09. Not long to go until the big break is coming, so one final push!
Last week, the amazing Tammy spent time doing some awesome diagrams to help you manipulate and play with linked lists. I hope that understanding how to use these wonderful data structures is starting to come together and the problem sets are helping you to manipulate linked lists more easily. In this coming week even more linked list fun :) On Monday, Tammy will do a linked list lecture program for you (close to your Assignment stuff, so a larger problem involving linked lists!) and on Wednesday I will be back to talk about the exam, with all the necessary information of what you need to know and how to approach your study for the exam.
The following week (Week 10) will focus on revision - we will be doing practice problems that are similar to what you may find in the exam and I will be running a poll to see which topics we want to focus on etc.
Lectures this week
Monday 11-1pm
Lecture 16:
Linked Lists lecture program
(slides available soon)
Wednesday 4-6pm
Lecture 17:
Exam Information
(slides available on Tuesday)
You will be able to find this week’s live code
here
.
myExperience Surveys
For those of you new to UNSW, the university runs
myExperience surveys
for all their students in each subject to provide feedback for the term - this is your chance to improve your courses, programs, and the overall UNSW student experience. We value your feedback greatly and use the data and the comments to improve the offering for the next cohort of students and to improve our teaching overall. The surveys are anonymous, and the results are only released after the marks are released for the term. Please make sure to
complete the survey
(opening at 9am Monday morning) to let us know how we did this term, what was something you loved about the course, and what was something that you felt could be improved - we love your feedback! 💗
Assignment 1 Marks
A quick update to let you know that we are finalising marks for Assignment 1 and they should be released early-mid this coming week!
I am keeping it short and sweet as I know everyone has reached the point of the term where you are all exhausted. Have a wonderful Week 9 - you are almost there! I am looking forward to seeing you all again in lectures on Wednesday (and Tammy on Monday :) ) !
Cheers,
Sasha and the awesome COMP1511 team 🌟

subject: [COMP6713] Notice: Guest Talks + Assignment Marking Methodology
body:
Aditya Joshi created a notice COMP6713 on Fri 17 April 2026 at 10:17:35 AM.
Guest Talks + Assignment Marking Methodology
Hello all,
This announcement contains details of the guest talks in week 10 and the assignment marking methodology.
(A) GUEST TALKS
In week 10, our lectures will be organised as follows:
Monday, 20th April:
Week 10 content (+Guest lecture by Dr. Mauel Weiss, JobAdder)
Wednesday, 22nd April:
Course recap, final exam details and discussion (+Guest lecture by Dr. Necva Bolucu, CSIRO)
You are strongly encouraged to attend in person if you would like to engage with the speakers live or ask questions about the final exam. Please note that the content of the guest lectures will not be included in the final exam syllabus. However, the week
10 content and course recap/final exam will be included in the syllabus for the final exam.
Talk 1:
The Model is Only 5%: Engineering Resilient AI Systems for the Real World by
Dr. Manuel Weiss (JobAdder)
This talk will discuss the practical challenges of deploying AI systems in real-world settings, with a focus on issues beyond the model itself. Topics will include latency, cost considerations, security, maintenance, evaluation, and other engineering factors
that are essential for building reliable AI systems in practice.
Dr. Manuel Weiss completed a PhD in Bioinformatics at the University of Zurich, and has worked as a software engineer across several companies. He spent around seven years at SEEK, where he led the recommendations team and later also the search team. He
is currently Director of Engineering at JobAdder, a recruitment software company.
----
Talk 2:
Tackling Noisy Annotations in Scientific Information Extraction by
Dr. Necva Bolucu (CSIRO)
In real-world annotation pipelines, perfectly labelled datasets are rare. Label noise can arise from disagreements among crowdsourced annotators or inconsistencies introduced by domain experts working under ambiguous guidelines. Regardless of its source,
this noise poses a serious challenge to model reliability. This issue is especially important in scientific text, where domain complexity is high and expert-quality annotation is expensive. Existing solutions remain limited, often either discarding noisy samples
and losing useful signal, or treating all annotations uniformly. This talk presents an adaptive approach that distinguishes clean samples from noisy ones and leverages both through weighted weakly supervised learning, showing consistent improvements across
scientific IE benchmarks.
Dr. Necva Bolucu is currently a Research Scientist at CSIRO. Her research focuses on natural language processing, deep learning, and the scientific domain. Her work includes information extraction, domain adaptation, knowledge extraction, and downstream
NLP applications in scientific text.
---
(B) ASSIGNMENT MARKING METHODOLOGY
The
manual grading
was conducted by the course team using the detailed marking rubric shared with the class. Graders had the freedom to err on the side of caution when deemed appropriate, and have also added their notes wherever applicable.
The
automatic grading
was conducted using 30 curated test cases, and the marking criteria were implemented using appropriate NLP techniques (and you have learned all of them in this course! :) ). The marks across each criterion are the average
of the test cases. The autograder ran successfully and produced non-zero marks for around 98% of submissions.
We have received assignment review requests from ~15 students, and plan to address them in late week 10/week 11. Any updates will be ref [ORDER_ID] in Moodle once the review is complete.
If you have reported a concern regarding receiving zero marks for auto-grading, we will also attempt to make
minor
changes to fix your code. However, as can be expected, you will only receive partial marks, in case the tests pass.
Please reach out to the cs6713 mailing list <[EMAIL]> in case of further issues.
--
I thank the tutors (and the course admin, of course!) for their meticulous effort marking the assignments.
I hope to see all of you in class in week 10! I wish you all the best for the group project and the final exam.
Regards,
Aditya

subject: MATH1081 Virtual Board Tutorial Trial EOI
body:
Dear student,
Thank you for your interest in this term's trial virtual board tutorials for MATH1081.
Unfortunately we will be unable to accommodate your participation in the trial. While we have made every effort to accommodate as many students as possible, we have implemented a strict technological requirement for this trial, requiring working camera, microphone, and the ability to handwrite digitally. Board tutorials are student-focused classes are require consistent active participation. These requirements are in place to ensure that participants have the best experience and the best chance to develop the same skills as students attending classes in person. If this term's trial is successful, we will revisit these requirements to any expanded offering made. Additionally, there is only one available timeslot for this trial and only students who are available at this time have been selected to participate. Lastly, we have prioritised students who indicated need for this trial. Priority was given in particular to students who indicated an ongoing need for remote participation. In this instance we were unable to accommodate students whose situations affected their capacity to participate in person for shorter periods.
Thank you for taking the time to express your interest and please do reach out if you have any questions or concerns about the course more broadly. I hope that you have a fantastic term in MATH1081 despite any disappointment.
Best,
Nathan

subject: [COMP6713] Notice: Assignment: Response to questions (1/n)
body:
Dipankar Srirag 📸 updated a notice COMP6713 on Fri 06 March 2026 at 04:29:55 PM.
Assignment: Response to questions (1/n)
Hello everyone,
It is great to see that you started working on the assignment, and have similar questions, particularly around what counts as
training data
and what external resources are allowed.
For this assignment, the dataset we provided (data.csv) should be used to build any
models, statistics, or heuristics
that help you identify which words in a sentence should be replaced. You are free to use any reasonable method for this part, but
please do not use large pretrained transformer models such as BERT, GPT, etc
.
Regarding
pretrained embeddings and lexical resources
, you are allowed to use tools such as
GloVe, gensim embeddings, spaCy (including models like en_core_web_sm), NLTK and WordNet
identify suitable replacement words
or compute semantic similarity. However, they should not be used as additional training data for your model.
Please also make sure that your submission is self-sufficient and runs without any manual changes to it when running automatic tests. Your submission should contain only:
zid.py
zid.pdf
packaged together in zid.zip.
Finally, we have uploaded a small set of unit tests on Moodle. The unit tests are provided
only for your own qualitative testing
, and you are allowed to add your examples to the file. You can use them to check whether your approach produces reasonable outputs. The automatic marking tests and the corresponding setup is not being
shared.
Hope this helps clarify things. If you still have questions, please feel free to post on the forum. All the best!
Regards,
Dipankar

COMP1511 –
Announcements
Week 8!
Hi Everyone and Welcome to Week 8 (well almost!) 😄
Hope you all enjoyed the introduction to Linked Lists last week with the amazing Tammy - I am sure her drawing skills impressed you all and the diagrams were next level. Tammy will continue through her epic slide deck (from Week 7) this coming week for Linked Lists. This a reminder that there is no lecture this coming Monday (I swear not an April Fool's Day thing, there is no lecture due to the public holiday) and just one lecture this week on Wednesday :) On Wednesday, Tammy will go over inserting anywhere in a list, considering edge cases in linked lists and also deleting from a linked list. When you are working through linked lists, try and draw lots of diagrams before you get into the problems. Linked lists also absolutely and utterly lend themselves to drawing diagrams - a good diagram here makes it so much easier to then link it up to a code translation! So I really want to see diagrams galore!
This Monday is a
public holiday
and I hope you are all enjoying an Easter long weekend at least a little bit amongst all your uni work. This means that we have shifted the due date of the Week 7 Problem Set to Tuesday of Week 8.
Lectures this week
Monday 11-1pm: Lecture 13: NO LECTURE DUE TO PUBLIC HOLIDAY
Wednesday 4-6pm
Lecture 14: Linked Lists (slides will be available in the next day or two)
You will be able to find this week's lecture code
here
.
Assignment 1
🌼🌸❀✿🌷
Assignment 1 marking has commenced, and we will keep you updated when the marks will be released (aiming for early/mid Week 9). A huge congratulations to you all for completing this assignment 🥳 - for some of you, this would have been your first large chunk of code that you have had to produce. I am sure there were many challenges along the way, and I hope that you enjoyed figuring out the problems and that you learned a lot about arrays and structs/enums along the way. Well done to you all!!!

🥞

Tuesday at 1:00PM (Tuesday week 08)
in hybrid mode - K17 SEM113 +

subject: Planned outage to HR System (PiMs), 8-9 November 2025
body:
Planned outage to HR System (PiMs), 8-9 November 2025
UNSW IT would like to advise you of a planned outage of the People Information Management System (PiMs) from 8am Saturday 8 November
until 5pm Sunday 9 November, while we undertake a security upgrade of our HR system.
How will this impact you?
You won’t be able to access all HR functions in PiMs, including via ‘My Staff Profile’
on
myUNSW
, during the outage. For example, you won’t be able to view payslips, submit, view or approve absences, or submit, view or approve timesheets.
We apologise for any inconvenience during this maintenance period. If you need HR support during this time, email the
HRD Business Systems
team.
UNSW IT
Please
restart your computer daily, to allow for software updates and to maximise performance.
You can contact UNSW IT by
raising a service ticket
,
visiting a
Campus IT Hub
,
calling the IT Service Centre on [PHONE], or sending us an
email
.

subject: [NAME] COMP1947 – Request for Support and Grade Clarification
body:
Dear COMP1947 Team,
I hope you're doing well. I’m reaching out because I just saw our group project mark and realized we didn’t do as well as I had hoped. I’m feeling pretty anxious, and I was wondering if there’s any way I could get a sense of where I currently stand in the course.
I want to say how much I’ve genuinely enjoyed COMP1947. It’s been one of the most interesting and challenging courses I’ve taken, and I’ve really appreciated how applicable and engaging the content has been. I’ve put in a lot of effort this term and tried to
stay on top of everything, but I honestly have no idea how I did on the final, and I’m really scared that it might not have gone well enough.
I need at least a 57 to pass and receive credit toward my major back home in the USA. If I don’t, I won’t be able to graduate on time. That’s why I’m writing. I’m feeling overwhelmed, and just really hoping for a little clarity or guidance on whether I’m on
track to pass.
I’ve loved this course and the way it’s deepened my understanding of machine learning as a CS major with a concentration in AI. Thank you for all the work you’ve put into teaching it. Any insight you can offer would mean the world right now.
Warmly,
[NAME]
z[PHONE]

subject: myUNSW system outage on Wednesday 31 July 💻
body:
Notice of planned myUNSW outage
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Planned system outage impacting myUNSW and other services on Wednesday 31 July
Hi Matthew,
What is happening?
As part of important planned updates, a scheduled outage is taking place from
5pm - 10pm on Wednesday 31 July.
This outage will impact your access to a number of UNSW systems and services, including myUNSW.
What is the impact?
During the outage you will not be able to access myUNSW key services such as:
accessing your academic statements
amending your enrolment
dropping a course
accessing the special consideration portal
paying fees.
What do I need to do?
To avoid any inconvenience, ensure you make any updates you need to make in myUNSW before 5pm on Wednesday 31 July.
Thank you for your understanding and cooperation during this period.

New enrolment feature!
Fetch your courses from myPlan, directly to your Enrolment Basket

We’re excited to introduce a new enhancement to the myUNSW Enrolment Basket, which allows you to seamlessly pull your planned courses from myPlan into your enrolments page.
You no longer need to switch between screens or manually search and select courses. Your course enrolment process is now faster and hassle-free!
How it works
1.
In
myPlan
, select the courses you plan to enrol in for your upcoming term.
2.
In
myUNSW
, go to the Course Enrolments page and select the 'Get myPlan Courses' button to pull across your planned courses from myPlan.
Need help?
For assistance with either of these steps, you can find comprehensive
Quick Reference [ORDER_ID]
on the myPlan webpage.
Please contact

subject: Learn how to protect yourself against scams 🔒
body:
Stay vigilant and report suspicious activity
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Matthew, do you know how to spot a scam?
Scammers can strike when you least expect them, tricking you into handing over money or personal details. Scammers are even pretending to be UNSW, resulting in some students losing thousands of dollars.
Take a minute to learn how to identify and respond to scams.
Recognise the red flags
🚩
It could be a scam if you're asked to pay your tuition fees:
Over the phone
On social media
By direct bank transfer
UNSW will never ask you to pay your fees via any method other than myUNSW.
Authentic emails from UNSW will:
Direct you to pay tuition fees on myUNSW  ✅
Come from an email address ending in @unsw.edu.au  ✅
Suspect a scam?
Take these steps to protect yourself, your family and other students.
1. Stop
Do not provide any information or money.
Hang up suspicious phone calls.
Do not click links in suspicious emails.
You can verify that a message has come from UNSW and not a scammer by contacting
The Nucleus: Student Hub
.
2. Report
Report suspected scams to
UNSW Protective Services
. Don't be afraid to report or seek advice. Speaking up protects you, your family and other students.
3. Get support
If you have been targeted, contact
Psychology and Wellness
or a
Student Support Advisor
.
Discover the types of scams that target UNSW students
Learn more about how to spot these scams, and what to do if you're targeted.
Learn more

subject: Have your say on the new academic calendar
body:
Take a quick survey for the chance to win a $50 or $100 voucher
Having trouble viewing this message?
View it online
instead!
Dear Matthew,
Welcome to UNSW in 2025. We hope you've had a restful break and are looking forward to the year ahead as much we we are.
Over the past year, we have been working to develop the best academic calendar for our university, now and for the future. As we move towards making a decision, we want to hear your thoughts.
Have your say
The University has explored many different options and narrowed them down to two calendars for consideration:
A semester calendar
A ref [ORDER_ID] trimester calendar
These options are based on years of student feedback from surveys and focus groups, providing a comprehensive picture of the student experience at UNSW.
However, we need your input to complete the picture. Share your thoughts on the academic calendar in a
short survey
. Your insights are essential to understanding your diverse experiences and priorities at UNSW.
The survey will close on
Wednesday 26 February
. It should only take a few minutes to complete.
By completing the survey and entering your details, you could win 1 of 5 $100 Coles/Myer gift vouchers or 1 of 5 $50 UNSW Grad Shop vouchers.
Please do also check out our possible
academic calendar models
and
share your thoughts
about the two options.
The final decision on our new academic calendar will be made in early 2025.
It will be rolled out from 2027 or 2028, depending on the calendar chosen.
I look forward to your input to help us build a calendar fit for the future for all of us.
Kind regards,
Vlado
Perkovic
Provost
